It keeps the door open for compiler-managed transparent multithreading on WASM — currently post-v1 (see [deferred.md § Compiler-Managed Transparent Threading on WASM](deferred.md#compiler-managed-transparent-threading-on-wasm)) — without requiring source-level changes when that lowering lands.

#### WASM Concurrency Lowering

On `wasm_browser` and `wasm_wasi`, `spawn`/`par`/channel operations lower by default to **sequential cooperative scheduling** on the main thread. No Web Workers, no SharedArrayBuffer, no COOP/COEP deployment constraints, no Web Worker startup latency. Smallest bundle, widest deployment profile.

For compute-bound workloads where single-thread performance is insufficient, `--features wasm-threads` opts into **shared-memory multithreading** using Web Workers + SharedArrayBuffer + atomics. This requires the user to deploy with COOP/COEP headers — a real-world constraint that belongs in the user's hands rather than as a default cost. Shared-memory multithreading is strictly additive: the same source compiles both ways; the opt-in swaps the lowering of `spawn` and channels from sequential to worker-based without changing any source.

| Build shape | `spawn` / channel lowering | User deployment action |
|---|---|---|
| `--target wasm_browser` (default) | Cooperative sequential on main thread | — |
| `--target wasm_browser --features wasm-threads` | Web Worker pool + SAB + atomics | Set COOP/COEP headers |
| `--target wasm_wasi` (default) | Host-provided threading if available, else sequential | Depends on host runtime |
| `--target native` | OS threads + runtime scheduler | — |

Ambitious shapes — compiler-managed transparent worker partitioning (ownership already proves data-race freedom, so no `--features` flag would be needed; the compiler infers the partition), WASM stack-switching for fiber-weight tasks over a small worker pool, and a design targeting the W3C shared-everything-threads proposal — are **deferred to post-v1** with concrete re-evaluation triggers in [deferred.md § Compiler-Managed Transparent Threading on WASM](deferred.md#compiler-managed-transparent-threading-on-wasm). The source-level commitments above keep those paths open without requiring a source break to land later.

#### Async Host APIs on WASM

Browser host APIs that return Promises (`fetch`, `crypto.subtle.digest`, IndexedDB operations) and callback-driven APIs (`setTimeout`, `requestAnimationFrame`, DOM event listeners) integrate with Kāra's concurrency primitives **without any new language surface**. The existing `suspends` effect, channel-receive, and the scheduler's yield-to-event-loop behavior cover the full use case.

**Promise-returning host APIs.** A `host fn` that wraps a Promise-returning host API declares `suspends` in its effect list. The call site looks like any other function call:

```kara
// stdlib, std.web.net
host fn fetch(url: ref String) -> Result[Response, HttpError]
    with sends(Network), receives(Network), suspends;

// user code
fn load_user(id: UserId) -> Result[User, Error] with suspends, ... {
    let resp = net.fetch(f"/users/{id}")?;
    resp.parse_json()
}
```

No `.await`, no `.then`, no `async` keyword. Consistent with Kāra's existing "no function coloring" stance (see [What Kāra Is Not](#what-kāra-is-not)): `suspends` propagates through the effect system; the scheduler handles yield bookkeeping.

**Callback-driven host APIs.** Timers, animation frames, and DOM events are modeled as **channel producers** in library wrappers on top of the `host fn` substrate. A library pattern:

```kara
// stdlib, std.web.time
pub fn after(duration: Duration) -> Receiver[()]
    with writes(Timer), allocates(Heap)
{
    let (tx, rx) = channel[()](1);
    host.set_timeout(|| tx.send(()), duration.as_ms());
    rx
}

// user code
fn delay_then(op: fn() -> ()) with writes(Timer), allocates(Heap), ... {
    after(Duration.ms(500)).recv();
    op();
}
```

Event streams (clicks, keypresses, pointer moves) use the same channel shape — the library wires the DOM listener to a channel sender; the user consumes via `channel.recv()` or iteration.

**Scheduler contract on WASM.** Channel-receive on a channel whose sender is host-async yields the waiting task and returns control to the browser event loop. Host-async completion (a Promise resolving, a `setTimeout` firing, an event dispatching) wakes the waiting task by posting to the scheduler's ready queue. The source-level semantics are identical to native: the function appears to block on the receive; the scheduler implements it as a yield without source-level ceremony.

**Why no `await` keyword in v1.** The effect system + scheduler cover the full use case without one; adding `await` would introduce a new form that interacts with the effect system, ownership, and target-gating surface area, with no offsetting value the existing machinery does not already deliver. A future `await` is deferred to post-v1 with concrete re-evaluation triggers in [deferred.md § `await` Keyword for Async APIs](deferred.md#await-keyword-for-async-apis) — the source surface is kept minimal today to avoid a locked-in keyword choice before real UI code has exercised the channel-based approach.

### Target Build Artifacts

`karac build --target T` produces a target-specific artifact set in `dist/<T>/`. The artifact contract is stable so downstream tooling (bundlers, loaders, runtimes, deployment pipelines) can integrate without per-project adaptation.

#### Native (`--target native`)

A single executable or static/dynamic library in `dist/native/<pkg>` (no extension on unix; `.exe` on Windows). Library builds emit the platform-native archive format. Nothing unusual — this is ordinary LLVM output.

#### WASM (`--target wasm_browser` or `--target wasm_wasi`)

The `karac build` command accepts a `--bindings` flag selecting the output shape:

```
karac build --target wasm_browser --bindings browser    # browser-ready
karac build --target wasm_wasi    --bindings component  # Component Model
karac build --target wasm_browser --bindings none       # raw .wasm only
```

**Default `--bindings` is inferred from the target.** `wasm_browser` defaults to `--bindings browser`; `wasm_wasi` defaults to `--bindings component`. Omitting `--bindings` is the common path. There is no universal default for `--target wasm` in the abstract — the `--target` choice already declares the host family, so defaulting off that choice avoids silent browser-lock-in.

**Browser bindings (`--bindings browser`).** Produces a flat directory:

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `dist/wasm/<pkg>.wasm` | The WASM module. |
| `dist/wasm/<pkg>.js` | ES-module glue with wasm-bindgen-compatible calling convention. Default loaders in vite / webpack / esbuild / rollup work without custom configuration. |
| `dist/wasm/<pkg>.d.ts` | TypeScript declarations for every exported function, including the JS-side shapes for `Result` / `Option` and any exported struct. |

*Shipped status:* the project-mode `dist/wasm/<pkg>.{wasm,js,d.ts}` layout is live (package name from `kara.toml`; single-file builds emit the same set as `<stem>.{wasm,js,d.ts}` in the CWD). The `.d.ts` today declares the glue module's full surface plus a `HostImpls` interface typing every `host fn` per the boundary contract; per-export function signatures (including the `Result`/`Option` shapes above) extend the generator when [entry point discovery](#entry-point-discovery) lands — `_start` is the only exported user entry point until then.

**Component Model bindings (`--bindings component`).** Produces a single Component Model file:

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `dist/wasm/<pkg>.wasm` | Component Model module with WIT interfaces embedded per the spec. Usable by wasmtime, jco, and other Component Model hosts. |

*Shipped status:* the **embedded-WIT single-component form is the live default** (phase-10 "embedded-WIT migration"; the gate — wasmtime/jco-class hosts running embedded-WIT components natively, with the paired-form wrapper no longer the common case — was re-evaluated and met 2026-06-06: wasmtime runs components with no flag, jco 1.0 is stable, and every comparable toolchain ships a single component as its artifact). Project mode lands `dist/wasm/<pkg>.wasm` (package name from `kara.toml`; single-file builds emit `<stem>.wasm` in the CWD); the artifact is a WASI 0.2 command component — the preview1 command adapter synthesizes `export wasi:cli/run` from the core module's `_start` — whose embedded world imports `kara:<pkg>/host` (every `host fn`, typed per the WIT boundary mapping — 64-bit ints ⇒ `s64`/`u64`, raw pointers ⇒ wasm32 `u32` addresses, opaque handles at their field's scalar width). `wasmtime run <pkg>.wasm` works directly. Per-export WIT signatures beyond the entry point extend the embedded world when [entry point discovery](#entry-point-discovery) lands.

The former **paired form** — `<pkg>.wasm` (C-ABI core module, `kara_host` imports) + `<pkg>.component.wit` (WIT interface descriptor) for wrapping by external tools — is **removed**: the `--bindings component-paired` spelling is now the standard unknown-value hard error. Both the swap and the removal landed pre-first-release, so the spec'd "deprecation notice one release before the swap / removal one release after" contract is discharged vacuously — no release ever carried the paired shape or its deprecation notice, and no downstream consumers existed at any point. The no-external-tool fallback is `--bindings none` (raw C-ABI core module). Migration is non-breaking at the source level — `host fn` declarations and effect contracts are unchanged.

**Raw `.wasm` (`--bindings none`).** Produces only `dist/wasm/<pkg>.wasm`. For users who want to write their own glue or target an unusual host.

#### Component Model emission

Kāra never bakes the Component Model spec into the compiler — that would couple compiler releases to a specification that is still evolving. The embedded-WIT default delegates the spec-coupled transform to `wasm-tools` as an **external tool**: `karac` renders the WIT world itself (plain text — `wit.rs`, no spec machinery), then shells out to `wasm-tools component embed` (host-fn builds only) and `wasm-tools component new`. The binary resolves from `KARAC_WASM_TOOLS` then `PATH`, and the package can pin the exact version in `kara.toml`:

```toml
[toolchain]
wasm-tools = "1.251.0"   # exact match against `wasm-tools --version`; drift is a hard error
```

Unpinned builds accept whatever version is discovered; a missing binary is a hard error naming the install recipe and the escape hatch (`--bindings none`, which needs no Component Model machinery at all). The one spec-adjacent data ingredient — the `wasi_snapshot_preview1` **command** adapter that lifts the preview1-ABI core module — is vendored into `karac` through the `wasi-preview1-component-adapter-provider` crate (wasmtime's own release artifact, pinned by karac's `Cargo.lock`); `KARAC_WASI_ADAPTER=<path>` substitutes an on-disk adapter. No wasi WIT files are vendored anywhere: the adapter contributes the `wasi:cli/run` export and the `wasi:*` imports at `component new` time. Migration to in-compiler Component Model emission is a post-v1 decision, dependent on spec stability.

#### Entry point discovery

Exported symbols in a WASM build are the public functions tagged for the corresponding target. A function is exported when all of the following hold:

1. It is `pub`.
2. It carries `#[target(wasm_browser)]` or `#[target(wasm_wasi)]` matching the build target *(or is target-agnostic and transitively reachable from a `#[target(wasm_...)]` entry point — that case is tracked by the linker's dead-code elimination in the usual way)*.
3. Its parameter and return types are expressible in the binding surface (`#[target(wasm_browser)]` requires primitives / `Copy` / opaque-handle types — same restriction as `host fn`; `#[target(wasm_wasi)]` can additionally use WIT-expressible types).

There is no separate `#[export]` attribute. `pub` + target tag is sufficient.

#### GPU (`--target gpu`)

Produces SPIR-V / WGSL per `#[gpu]` kernel; see [GPU Subset Constraints](#gpu-subset-constraints). Not a standalone deployable binary — GPU kernels are consumed by a host program via `gpu.dispatch`.

#### Future consolidation — package manifest

A `.karapack` manifest format describing the artifact set (modules, exports, embedded WIT, declared effect requirements per export, toolchain version) is a post-v1 possibility tracked in `docs/deferred.md § Package Manifest Artifact Format`. It would replace the per-file-naming convention above with a structured, tool-consumable descriptor. Deferring the manifest lets downstream tooling surface what it actually wants from a descriptor before the shape is frozen.

---

## Project Profiles

Project-wide constraints declared in `kara.toml`. Profiles map to sets of effect restrictions enforced by the compiler across the entire project — no per-function annotations needed.

```toml
[profile]
name = "kernel"
no_effects = ["allocates(Heap)", "panics"]
no_std = true
panic_handler = "custom"
```

**Built-in profiles:**

| Profile | Restrictions | Use case |
|---|---|---|
| `kernel` | No heap allocation, no panics, no RC, no std | OS kernels, hypervisors |
| `embedded` | No heap allocation, no panics, no RC, no std, no concurrency | Microcontrollers, interrupt handlers |

**Memory safety properties under restricted profiles.** The `embedded` and `isr` profiles may weaken two of the five memory-safety properties defined in § Starting Assumptions — explicitly, never silently:

- **Spatial safety (bounds checking):** `embedded`/`isr` profiles may disable slice bounds-checking panics via `bounds_checks = false` in `kara.toml`. This is an explicit opt-in the profile author takes responsibility for. Without it, bounds checking is active even on embedded targets.
- **Integer overflow behavior:** `embedded` profile defaults to `wrapping` arithmetic rather than panicking. This is still defined behavior (never UB), but the behavior changes. The profile declaration makes this visible; individual projects may override to `checked` if they prefer.

**Profile flag — `panic_on_alloc_failure`.** Default `true`. Settable to `false` in any profile that permits heap allocation (i.e., not in `embedded` / `kernel` / `isr`, where heap is forbidden outright via `no_effects = ["allocates(Heap)"]`). Under `panic_on_alloc_failure = false`, panicking allocator methods (`Vec.push`, `String.push`, `Box.new`, the collection literal forms, etc.) are rejected at call sites in favor of their `try_*` Result-returning companions; auto-RC fallback emission becomes a typecheck error rather than a silent runtime fallback; `#[derive(Clone)]` on alloc-bearing types is rejected. The flag is orthogonal to other profile knobs — the canonical use case is a project running under the `app` profile that wants explicit OOM handling for real-time, fault-tolerant, or kernel-with-allocator workloads. See [Fallible Allocation API and OOM Handling](#fallible-allocation-api-and-oom-handling) for the full method-per-method spec, the `AllocError` prelude type, and the diagnostic shapes.

Temporal safety, type safety, and data race freedom are not weakened by any built-in profile — they hold on all targets in safe code. `unsafe` blocks are the only escape from these guarantees, and they require explicit annotation.

**Recursion in profiles.** GPU code forbids recursion entirely — GPU hardware has no call stack, so recursive calls cannot be lowered. The `kernel` and `embedded` profiles do **not** ban recursion: embedded hardware has a real call stack, and bounded recursion (tree walks, parser descents, state machines) is a legitimate pattern even on small MCU stacks. Deep recursion is impractical on typical embedded targets (2–16 KB stack), but that is a runtime constraint, not a language-level prohibition. Projects that want to ban recursion in embedded code can add `no_recursion = true` to a custom profile.

**Custom profiles** are supported from day one — a profile is just a named list of effect restrictions, so user-defined profiles cost nothing extra:

```toml
[profile]
name = "isr"                           # interrupt service routine
no_effects = ["allocates(Heap)", "panics", "writes(FileSystem)"]
no_std = true
```

A `deterministic` profile — useful for replayable simulations, offline batch jobs, build-system tooling, and any code where every run must produce byte-identical output — is just a bundle of forbidden nondeterminism resources, built from the resources named in [Nondeterminism as an Explicit Resource](#nondeterminism-as-an-explicit-resource). No new language feature is needed:

```toml
[profile]
name = "deterministic"
no_effects = ["reads(Clock)", "reads(RandomSource)", "reads(Env)"]
```

Under this profile, any function that transitively reads the wall clock, draws from the system RNG, or touches environment variables is a compile error. Tests that want determinism can still use `#[with_provider]` to inject fake clocks and fake RNGs without enabling the profile project-wide; the profile is for shipping binaries where determinism must hold by construction, not just by test discipline.

The compiler treats a profile as project-wide `#[no_effect(...)]` applied to every function — public and private. Effect checking is transitive, so restricting only public functions would allow violations to hide inside private call chains. Violations are compile errors with the same diagnostics as per-function effect violations. There is no `#[allow]` override; the only escape hatch is `unsafe { ... }`.

**Composing profiles.** A project may enable more than one profile at once by listing them in `kara.toml`:

```toml
profiles = ["embedded", "isr"]
```

The effective constraint set is the union: every `no_effects` entry across all enabled profiles is forbidden, and boolean flags like `no_std` are required if any enabled profile requires them. Libraries do not declare profile membership — a library is compatible with a profile iff its reachable functions' declared effects are disjoint from the profile's forbidden set, which the effect checker already verifies transitively. The same library therefore works under any profile whose constraints it satisfies, without per-profile annotations. This is deliberately not a general-purpose constraint system: profiles are named bundles over a closed taxonomy (forbidden effects plus a small set of boolean flags), so composition is set union on atoms the effect checker already understands — no fixpoint, no subtyping, no solver.

`engine` and `server` profiles are reserved names — they will be defined when their underlying features (SIMD auto-optimization, observability hooks, hot-reload) are implemented. Using them before implementation is a compile error.

---

## Panic Strategy: `panic = "unwind" | "abort"`

Project-level setting in `kara.toml` controlling how the `panics` effect compiles at codegen. The setting is part of the `[profile]` block alongside `name`, `no_effects`, `bounds_checks`, `panic_on_alloc_failure`, and the other per-profile knobs. Two values:

- **`unwind`** — panic walks the stack, runs `defer`/`errdefer`/`Drop` cleanup at every frame, can be caught by `catch_panic[T]`. Requires DWARF unwind tables, personality function, and cleanup landing pads at every frame containing cleanup. The default for `app` and `lib` profiles.
- **`abort`** — panic compiles to a single call to the panic handler (default or custom), then `abort()` syscall. **No cleanup runs.** `catch_panic[T]` and `extern "C-unwind"` are unavailable. The default for `embedded`, `kernel`, `isr`, `gpu` profiles.

```toml
[profile]
name = "small_server"
panic = "abort"             # opt into abort on the default-unwind app profile
```

**Per-profile defaults.**

| Profile | Default `panic` | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `app` | `unwind` | Cleanup matters in user-space programs; `catch_panic` available; FFI unwind works. |
| `lib` | `unwind` | Libraries should preserve options for consumers — recompiled per-binary anyway. |
| `embedded` | `abort` | No DWARF, no unwinder runtime on MCU targets. |
| `kernel` | `abort` | Ring-0 has no unwinding infrastructure. |
| `isr` | `abort` | Interrupt context cannot unwind; must run in bounded time. |
| `gpu` | `abort` | GPU has no panic mechanism at all (also moot — `panics` effect typically forbidden via `no_effects`). |

Custom profiles inherit `unwind` unless they explicitly set `panic = "abort"`. Profiles that already forbid the `panics` effect entirely (`no_effects = ["panics"]` in the canonical `embedded` / `kernel` / `isr` definitions) leave the `panic` setting as a fallback for runtime-internal panics that the language can't statically prevent (allocator failures the user opted into despite no-heap, FFI propagation, double-panic on the abort path).

**Cleanup contract under `panic = "abort"` — load-bearing semantic difference.** Under `panic = "unwind"`, the language guarantees that `defer`/`errdefer`/`Drop` blocks run on every panic path. Under `panic = "abort"`, **none of those blocks run on panic** — the panic handler runs once, then `abort()` is called. The "cleanup is guaranteed" contract has exactly **two** named exceptions:

1. **Double-panic** — a panic during a destructor that is itself running due to a prior panic. The process aborts immediately; cleanup blocks not yet reached are not run. This is a code-level event the user can avoid by writing panic-free destructors (the effect checker enforces `panics`-freedom on `Drop` bodies — see [Destructors](#drop-and-destructors)).
2. **`panic = "abort"`** — every panic, not just double-panic, terminates without running per-frame cleanup. This is a configuration-level choice the user makes at the profile level.

Both are explicit user-visible choices. The user who picks `panic = "abort"` is committing to "panics terminate the process without running my `defer`/`errdefer` blocks"; the language makes the choice visible at the configuration boundary so it can never be a surprise at the call site.

**The custom panic handler is the only user code that runs on panic under abort.** Users who need to release a hardware handle, feed a watchdog, dump telemetry, or otherwise coordinate a clean shutdown set `panic_handler = "custom"` in the profile and define a panic handler:

```toml
[profile]
name = "telemetry_server"
panic = "abort"
panic_handler = "custom"
```

```kara
#[panic_handler]
fn on_panic(info: ref PanicInfo) {
    Watchdog.feed_one_last_time();
    eprintln(f"PANIC: {info.message} at {info.location}");
    // returns; runtime calls abort() after this returns
}
```

The handler receives `ref PanicInfo` (the same struct `catch_panic` returns under `unwind`) and runs once for the entire program — *not* per-frame. This is structurally cleaner than per-frame `defer` for the abort-path use case: cleanup-on-panic-under-abort needs a single coordination point (release-the-watchdog, write-the-crash-file), not distributed cleanup at every stack frame. The handler must not panic — its own `panics` effect is rejected at the impl site (the same rule that already governs `Drop` bodies); a panic in the handler would be unrecoverable. The handler returns normally, and the runtime calls `abort()` after it returns; the handler cannot resume execution. If the handler diverges (returns `Never`), the runtime still calls `abort()` if execution somehow reaches the runtime side again — defensive, since divergence-then-return is undefined-behavior territory.

The default panic handler under `panic = "abort"` (when `panic_handler = "custom"` is not set) writes the panic site, panic kind, and panic message to stderr in a single `write(2)` call, then calls `abort()`. No JSON crash report, no stack trace — those require the unwinder runtime and `panic = "abort"` is the choice that opts out of it.

**Rejection rules — what's unavailable under `panic = "abort"`.** Pulled together for reference; the individual rules are specced in their own sections:

- **`catch_panic[T]`** — unavailable. Calls produce `error[E_CATCH_PANIC_REQUIRES_UNWIND]: catch_panic is unavailable under panic = "abort"; remove the call or change the build profile`. (Spec: [Catching Panics](#catching-panics-catch_panict).)
- **`extern "C-unwind"`** — unavailable. Both declarations and calls are rejected at parse with `error[E_EXTERN_C_UNWIND_REQUIRES_UNWIND_PROFILE]`. (Spec: [FFI](#ffi).)
- **`process.exit(code)`** — still available; under abort, it lowers to a direct `abort()` with the exit code recorded in the panic record. No unwind, no `defer` blocks run. Behavior is consistent with the cleanup-contract rule above.
- **`try { ... }` blocks** — unaffected; they use `Result`, not panic absorption. (Spec: [Try Blocks](#try-blocks-try---).)

**Codegen.** Under `panic = "unwind"`, the compiler emits LLVM `invoke` instructions at every call site that may panic, with a personality function driving cleanup landing pads at every frame containing `defer`/`errdefer`/RC-decrements/`Drop`. DWARF `.eh_frame` and `.gcc_except_table` sections are emitted; the unwinder runtime (`libkarac_runtime`'s unwind shim, layered over `libunwind` or the platform equivalent) drives the walk. Under `panic = "abort"`, the compiler emits plain `call` instructions everywhere — no `invoke`, no personality function, no cleanup pads, no DWARF unwind tables. Panic-emitting primitives (`unwrap`, `expect`, index-out-of-bounds, divide-by-zero, explicit `panic()`) compile to a call to `__kara_panic_handler(info)` followed by an unconditional `__kara_abort()` (which the optimizer marks `noreturn`). The abort path is unreachable per the runtime contract, so the LLVM optimizer aggressively dead-code-eliminates everything past the panic site.

**Library packages may declare `panic` in their `kara.toml`** but the setting is **advisory**. Kāra has no ABI-stable library distribution at v1 — every library is recompiled per-binary against the leaf binary's active profile. A library declaring `panic = "abort"` documents intent ("I expect to be linked into an abort binary; my code may rely on cleanup not running") but does not bind. When a library declaring `panic = "abort"` is consumed by a binary using `panic = "unwind"` (or vice versa), the library code is recompiled under the binary's setting; the build emits a warning at link time:

```
warning[W_PANIC_STRATEGY_MISMATCH]: package 'foo' declares panic = "abort"; this build uses panic = "unwind". Library code recompiled under unwind. If 'foo' relies on abort semantics for correctness (e.g., omits cleanup that would otherwise run), review the package's documentation.
```

The leaf binary's setting always wins. The library setting is a hint to the human reader of `kara.toml`, not a constraint on linking. Libraries that *require* a specific panic strategy for correctness are vanishingly rare — most code is correct under both — and the warning surfaces the rare case for review without forcing a hard error.

**Why abort is not the default for `app`/`lib`.** Most user-space programs benefit from unwind: cleanup runs (file handles close, locks release, RC counts decrement), `catch_panic` is available for panic-isolation at scheduler / FFI boundaries, `extern "C-unwind"` works for FFI exception propagation, panic-debugging is easier (the unwind walks every frame so the panic-report tool has full stack visibility, including the `Cleanup chain` field per [Crash Report Format](#4-crash-report-format)). The binary-size cost — DWARF unwind tables and per-frame cleanup pads — is acceptable for non-embedded code, typically 10-20% of binary size. Abort is reserved for the embedded/kernel cases where the runtime infrastructure isn't available, plus opt-in for users who want smaller binaries and accept the trade-offs.

**Composing `panic = "abort"` with `panic_on_alloc_failure = false`.** The two flags are orthogonal and compose freely. A `panic = "abort"` project that wants explicit OOM handling (rather than abort-on-OOM) sets both: `panic = "abort"` rejects `catch_panic` and runs no cleanup on the panic path; `panic_on_alloc_failure = false` rejects panicking allocator methods so allocation failure surfaces as `Result[T, AllocError]` rather than reaching the panic path at all. The combination is the natural shape for fault-tolerant systems that can't afford either an unwind runtime or unrecoverable allocation failures.

---

## AI-First Compiler Interface

Three v1 commitments that make Kāra a language *designed to be written by AI*:

### 1. Structured Compiler Output

All diagnostics (errors, warnings, performance notes, concurrency reports) available as JSON with machine-applicable fix diffs. AI agents apply fixes mechanically — no human-in-the-loop needed for routine fixes like effect annotation updates, ownership adjustments, or missing pattern arms:

```bash
$ karac build --output=json
{
  "program_effects": ["reads(FileSystem, Env, Stdin)", "writes(Stdout, Stderr, UserDB)", "sends(Network)"],  // null if build did not reach effect analysis
  "diagnostics": [{
    "id": "d1",
    "severity": "error", "primary": true,
    "code": "E0500", "category": "ownership",
    "concept": "ownership/move-semantics",
    "file": "src/order.kara", "line": 42, "column": 9,
    "message": "`user` moved on line 38, used again here.",
    "hints": [{
      "description": "clone `user` before the move",
      "diff": {"file": "src/order.kara", "line": 37,
               "old": "process(user);", "new": "process(user.clone());"}
    }]
  }, {
    "id": "d2",
    "severity": "error", "primary": true,
    "code": "E0502", "category": "effects",
    "concept": "effects/public-function-declaration",
    "file": "src/order.kara", "line": 12, "column": 1,
    "message": "public function `process` effect declaration incomplete",
    "inferred_effects": ["reads(UserDB)", "writes(Cache)"],
    "declared_effects": ["reads(UserDB)"],
    "derivation": [
      {"file": "src/order.kara", "line": 12, "reason": "process calls update_user"},
      {"file": "src/user.kara",  "line": 47, "reason": "update_user calls cache.store"},
      {"file": "src/cache.kara", "line": 89, "reason": "cache.store has writes(Cache)"}
    ],
    "hints": [{
      "description": "add missing effect to declaration",
      "diff": {"file": "src/order.kara", "line": 12,
               "old": "pub fn process(id: u64) -> Report with reads(UserDB) {",
               "new": "pub fn process(id: u64) -> Report with reads(UserDB) writes(Cache) {"}
    }]
  }]
}
```

#### Diagnostic Quality Commitments

The JSON document above is the compiler's sole output channel for signal. Four rules govern its shape:

**1. Channel separation — signal on stdout, noise on stderr.** The JSON diagnostic document is written to stdout and contains only actionable information: errors, warnings, performance notes, concurrency reports, and the `program_effects` summary. Build progress ("Compiling foo", "Linking..."), phase timings, cache-hit messages, and similar build-process instrumentation are written to stderr in human-readable form. AI agents read stdout and parse one JSON document with nothing to filter past. Developers running `karac build` interactively see progress on stderr. Phase timings and profiling data, when requested, go to a separate file (`--emit-timings=timings.json`), never mixed into the diagnostic stream.

**2. Root-cause grouping via `primary` and `consequence_of`.** Every diagnostic carries `"primary": true | false` and an `"id"`. Consequence diagnostics — those caused by a prior root-cause error, e.g. `user.name` is inaccessible because `user` was moved earlier — carry `"primary": false` and `"consequence_of": "<id>"` pointing at their root. AI agents fix primaries first; consequences often resolve themselves once the root is fixed.

```json
{ "id": "d1", "primary": true,  "code": "E0500", "message": "`user` moved on line 38..." }
{ "id": "d2", "primary": false, "consequence_of": "d1",
  "message": "`user.name` not accessible: `user` already moved" }
```

**3. Cascade cap of 20 primary errors per file.** Once the cap is reached for a file, the compiler emits a `"truncated"` marker and stops reporting further diagnostics *for that file*; other files continue reporting normally. Consequence diagnostics do not count against the cap. The cap applies only to `severity: "error"` — warnings, performance notes, and concurrency reports are never capped, because they describe the state of a working program and AI agents acting on them want the complete set in one pass.

```json
{ "truncated": { "file": "src/order.kara", "suppressed_primary_errors": 14 } }
```

Why a hard cap rather than stop-on-first-error: AI agents batch-apply independent fixes in one pass. 20 primaries is enough headroom for parallel fixing, small enough to avoid the "wall of errors" problem.

**4. `concept` field linking to spec anchors.** Every diagnostic carries `"concept": "<spec-section-anchor>"` (e.g. `"ownership/rc-fallback"`, `"effects/inference-boundaries"`, `"performance/allocation-hoisting"`). The anchor resolves against `docs/design.md`'s table of contents — AI agents fetch the authoritative spec section for context without guessing or web lookups. No URLs are used because URLs rot; anchors are part of the spec contract and stable across versions.

**5. `derivation` chain for inferred diagnostics.** Diagnostics that arise from transitive inference — effect inference, ownership analysis, type inference, RC-fallback decisions, parallelization decisions — must include an ordered `"derivation"` field: a list of `{file, line, reason}` tuples showing the chain of reasoning the compiler followed. For effect errors, this is the call chain that accumulated the unexpected effect. For ownership, it is the dataflow path that forced an RC fallback. For performance notes, the derivation *is* the content of the diagnostic — a bare "this allocates on every iteration" is not actionable; the chain showing *why* is.

Per-category applicability:

| Commitment                  | Errors         | Warnings       | Perf notes     | Concurrency reports |
|-----------------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|---------------------|
| `primary` / `consequence_of`| required       | optional       | N/A            | N/A                 |
| Cascade cap (20 / file)     | errors only    | no cap         | no cap         | no cap              |
| `concept` field             | required       | required       | required       | required            |
| `derivation` chain          | when inferred  | when inferred  | always         | always              |

**Error return traces.** When `?` propagates a `Result.Err` through multiple call frames, the compiler (in debug builds) inserts bookkeeping at each `?` site to record the propagation path. The trace appears alongside the diagnostic in `--output=json` and as a note in terminal output. It complements stack traces: a stack trace shows *where execution was*; an error trace shows *where the error traveled*.

```json
{
  "diagnostics": [{
    "severity": "runtime_error",
    "message": "Err(DbError.ConnectionTimeout) propagated to top level",
    "error_return_trace": [
      { "file": "src/db.kara",   "line": 84, "expr": "conn.query(sql)?" },
      { "file": "src/repo.kara", "line": 31, "expr": "db.find_user(id)?" },
      { "file": "src/api.kara",  "line": 12, "expr": "repo.get_user(id)?" }
    ]
  }]
}
```

Rules:

- **Debug builds only.** The compiler strips trace bookkeeping in `--release`. No configuration knob — this is a development tool.
- **Implementation:** A thread-local fixed-depth ring buffer (depth TBD, default 64 frames) of `(file, line, expr_text)` triples. Each `?` site in debug mode calls a compiler-generated intrinsic that pushes to the buffer on `Err` and clears it on `Ok`.
- **`Cancelled` is excluded.** The `Cancelled` variant in auto-concurrency is structural, not a user error. `?` propagation of `Cancelled` does not push a frame onto the ring buffer — the intrinsic skips the push when the `Err` variant is `Cancelled`. This prevents cancellation noise from polluting traces that should show the originating error's propagation path. Only the originating branch's error contributes trace frames.
- **Depth limit.** If the ring buffer is full, the oldest entry is dropped and a `"trace_truncated": true` flag is set in the JSON output. The trace always shows the most recent frames.
- **No async interaction yet.** Cross-task `?` propagation (if Kāra adds async) is deferred to Phase 6 (P0 — depends on concurrency runtime; see [Deferred Items](#deferred-items) — Cross-task `?` propagation).

### 1b. Streaming Phase Events

This protocol is meaningful only because the Compilation Model (§ Compilation Model) commits to named, observable pipeline phases. The phases listed below correspond one-to-one with the pipeline stages named there.

The batch `--output=json` document is emitted once, at the end of a build. For an AI client iterating rapidly — or for any consumer that wants to react to phase N's result while phase N+1 is still running — Kāra also supports a streaming output mode that emits one event per line as the build progresses. The streaming mode is additive: everything the batch mode produces is also present in the streaming mode, and a client can reconstruct the batch document from a complete streaming log.

**Invocation.** `karac build --output=jsonl` selects streaming mode. The value name follows the widely-used "JSON Lines" convention — newline-delimited JSON, one complete object per line.

**Format.** Each line is a single JSON object, terminated by `\n` (not CRLF), UTF-8 encoded, no pretty-printing. Each line has a `"type"` field naming the event. There is no wrapper object, no surrounding array — just a sequence of independent lines. This is `tail -f`-friendly and parseable line-by-line by streaming JSON libraries.

```
{"type":"build_start","build_id":"01HQ...","timestamp":"2026-04-11T10:23:04Z"}
{"type":"phase_start","phase":"lex","scope":{"files":["src/main.kara"]}}
{"type":"phase_complete","phase":"lex","errors":0,"warnings":0,"notes":0}
{"type":"phase_start","phase":"parse"}
{"type":"diagnostic","phase":"parse","id":"d1","primary":true,"severity":"error","code":"E0099","message":"unexpected token `}`","file":"src/main.kara","line":12,"column":3}
{"type":"phase_complete","phase":"parse","errors":1,"warnings":0,"notes":0}
{"type":"phase_skipped","phase":"typecheck","reason":"parse errors in input","blocking":["d1"]}
{"type":"phase_skipped","phase":"effect","reason":"typecheck did not run","blocking":["d1"]}
{"type":"phase_skipped","phase":"ownership","reason":"typecheck did not run","blocking":["d1"]}
{"type":"build_complete","build_id":"01HQ...","success":false,"total_errors":1,"total_warnings":0,"program_effects":null}
```

**Event types (the six-event minimum set).**

| Event | Cardinality | Payload |
|---|---|---|
| `build_start` | once per build | `build_id`, `timestamp`, CLI args echoed |
| `phase_start` | once per observable phase | `phase`, optional `scope` (files, functions) |
| `phase_complete` | once per observable phase that ran | `phase`, `errors`, `warnings`, `notes` |
| `phase_skipped` | zero or more | `phase`, `reason`, `blocking` — an array of diagnostic `id`s from prior phases that forced the skip |
| `diagnostic` | zero or more | Same shape as an entry in batch `--output=json`'s `diagnostics` array, plus a new `phase` field identifying which phase emitted it |
| `build_complete` | once per build | `build_id`, `success`, `total_errors`, `total_warnings`, `program_effects` (null if the build did not reach effect analysis) |

Sub-phase iteration events (monomorphization requests, SCC fixed-point rounds, cache hits, individual LLVM optimization passes) are deliberately **not** part of the streaming protocol. They flood the stream and are internal details. If future tooling needs them, they are emitted to separate files following the `--emit-timings=timings.json` pattern, never mixed into the diagnostic stream.

**Observable phases.** One event pair (`phase_start` / `phase_complete`, or `phase_skipped`) per named pipeline stage in the Compilation Model:

```
lex → parse → resolve → typecheck → effect → ownership → concurrency → codegen
```

An observable phase is a named pipeline stage from the Compilation Model section. Internal iteration within a phase (SCC fixed-point rounds, monomorphization requests, LLVM optimization passes) is **not** observable. Clients must accept phase names they do not recognize and treat them as opaque pipeline stages — new phases can be added in later Kāra versions without breaking old clients.

**Fail-fast semantics — the predecessor-usable-output rule.** Each phase runs unless its immediate predecessor produced zero usable output. "Usable" is phase-specific:

- `lex` — always runs (entry point).
- `parse` — runs even on lex errors; the parser consumes the error-token stream via recovery.
- `resolve` — runs iff parser produced a non-empty AST for at least one top-level item. Otherwise skipped.
- `typecheck` — runs iff resolve produced any successfully-resolved items. Unresolved names cascade as typecheck errors, which is expected and fine.
- `effect` — runs iff typecheck produced any typed items. Effect inference over untyped code is meaningless.
- `ownership` — runs iff typecheck produced any typed items (does not strictly depend on effect output).
- `concurrency` — runs iff both `effect` and `ownership` completed.
- `codegen` — runs iff **all** analysis phases completed with zero errors.

When a phase is skipped it emits `phase_skipped` carrying a `reason` string and a `blocking` array of diagnostic IDs from prior phases that forced the skip. The client sees exactly which earlier diagnostics cut the build short.

**Client-decides-when-to-stop.** There is no `--stop-on-first-error` flag. An LLM client that wants fail-fast behavior stops consuming at the first `phase_complete` with `errors > 0`; a client that wants the full cascade-capped report reads through to `build_complete`. The compiler keeps running as long as stdout has a reader — different clients want different amounts. If the client disconnects, stdout pipe backpressure naturally pauses the compiler; no explicit cancellation protocol is needed.

**Cascade cap preservation.** The 20-primary-errors-per-file cap from batch mode (§ 1, rule 3) applies identically in streaming mode. A file that hits the cap emits its `truncated` marker as a `diagnostic` event inside whichever phase hit the cap.

**Superset guarantee.** Streaming output is a strict superset of batch output. Given a complete JSONL log, a client reconstructs the batch JSON document by:

1. Initializing `diagnostics = []`, then appending every `diagnostic` event in stream order.
2. Taking `program_effects` from the final `build_complete` event.
3. Optionally populating a new `phases` array from the `phase_complete` and `phase_skipped` events, for clients that want per-phase summaries.

Four hard invariants:

1. Every diagnostic in batch mode appears in streaming mode, at the emitting phase's boundary.
2. Every diagnostic in streaming mode appears in batch mode.
3. `build_complete.total_errors` equals the sum of `phase_complete.errors` across all phases that ran (skipped phases contribute zero because they run no checks).
4. Cascade cap behavior is identical across modes — same file, same 20-primary limit, same `truncated` marker shape.

**Corollary — batch mode gains a `phase` field on every diagnostic.** This is a non-breaking addition (existing batch clients ignore unknown fields) and it means a single client library can consume either mode and produce the same in-memory representation.

### 2. Compiler Query API

Programmatic access to compiler analysis:

```bash
$ karac query effects src/order.kara.process_order        # inferred + declared effects
$ karac query ownership src/order.kara.process_order      # parameter modes, RC values
$ karac query concurrency src/order.kara.load_dashboard   # parallelization decisions
$ karac query layout src/types.kara.ParticleBatch         # chosen field order, SoA decomposition, repr
$ karac query cost-summary src/order.kara                 # per-fn + per-module static counts of every silent runtime cost the compiler emitted
$ karac query monomorphization src/order.kara             # per-generic instantiation counts + per-instance (T, E) tuples
```

Returns structured JSON. AI agents query directly instead of compile-parse-guess cycles.

**Inference queryability is a commitment, not a convenience.** Every major inferred decision — effects, body-level ownership usage classification (and the would-be tighter mode it implies), RC fallback, parallelization, layout, cumulative cost, monomorphization surface — must be reachable via `karac query`, not only surfaced when it produces a diagnostic. (Parameter modes themselves are *declared* per Part 1, not inferred — what the ownership query reports is the body-level classification feeding would-be-mode diagnostics and the RC fallback decision.) Kāra's pitch is "the programmer declares *what*, the compiler decides *how*"; that only works if the programmer can ask the compiler what it decided. Each query returns both the decision and a `derivation` chain explaining *why* (same shape as diagnostic derivations — see commitment 5 under Structured Compiler Output). A compiler pass that makes an inferred decision without a corresponding query surface is considered incomplete.

**Scope — batch, one-shot, narrow surface.** The three queries above are a batch interface: the client invokes `karac query ...`, the compiler runs enough of the pipeline to answer the question, and the answer comes back once. There is no subscribe model, no reactive re-computation on file edits, and no broader query surface (e.g., "type of this expression at this position", "visible trait impls here", "monomorphizations of this generic"). A full reactive query-based compilation model — rust-analyzer class, with a subscribe protocol, incremental re-computation driven by file watchers, and a broad query surface — is explicitly a **v2+ direction, not a v1 commitment**. The function-local analysis principles in the Compilation Model section are the substrate that would make such a layer feasible later, but building it now would double compiler complexity for a prototype that does not yet need it.

**`karac query concurrency` output schema.** Returns the compiler's full parallelization graph for a function — every call, which stage it runs in, why calls are sequential or parallel, and (where applicable) what the programmer would need to declare to unlock parallelism:

```json
{
  "function": "load_dashboard",
  "stages": [
    {
      "type": "parallel_group",
      "calls": [
        {"call": "fetch_profile(user_id)", "line": 2, "effects": ["reads(UserDB)"]},
        {"call": "fetch_orders(user_id)",  "line": 3, "effects": ["reads(OrderDB)"]},
        {"call": "fetch_notifs(user_id)",  "line": 4, "effects": ["reads(NotifDB)"]}
      ],
      "derivation": [
        {"reason": "no data dependencies between fetch_profile, fetch_orders, fetch_notifs"},
        {"reason": "no effect conflicts — different resources, all reads"}
      ]
    },
    {
      "type": "sync_point",
      "derivation": [
        {"reason": "build_dashboard takes return values of all three fetches"}
      ]
    },
    {
      "type": "sequential",
      "call": "build_dashboard(profile, orders, notifications)",
      "line": 7,
      "effects": []
    }
  ],
  "serialized_pairs": []
}
```

Each serialized pair includes a `"kind"` tag (`"data_dependency"` or `"effect_conflict"`) and a `"would_parallelize_if"` hint — `null` when the ordering is semantically required (read-after-write, data dependency); a description of the declaration needed (e.g., `independent` resources) when parallelism is blocked only by conservative defaults:

```json
"serialized_pairs": [
  {
    "a": "save_user(user)",
    "b": "fetch_user(user.id)",
    "kind": "effect_conflict",
    "conflict": "writes(UserDB) vs reads(UserDB)",
    "would_parallelize_if": null
  },
  {
    "a": "write_audit_log(event)",
    "b": "update_record(id, data)",
    "kind": "effect_conflict",
    "conflict": "writes(AuditDB) vs writes(UserDB)",
    "would_parallelize_if": "declare `independent AuditDB, UserDB;` — safe if audit and user tables are on separate connections"
  }
]
```

`"would_parallelize_if"` is a hint string, not a machine-applicable diff — declaring resources `independent` has semantic implications the programmer must verify. Machine-applicable diffs (e.g., missing effect annotations) appear only in `karac build --output=json`.

**`karac query cost-summary` output schema.** Returns a per-function and per-module aggregate of every silent runtime cost the compiler emitted, keyed by the categories enumerated under [Performance Diagnostics > Cumulative Cost Surface](#cumulative-cost-surface). v1 reports *static counts* — the compiler's own bookkeeping of how many sites of each kind it emitted, plus a `derivation` array of source locations:

```json
{
  "scope": "src/order.kara",
  "totals": {
    "rc_ops":                 {"count": 12, "rc": 9, "arc": 3},
    "arc_provider_wraps":     2,
    "borrow_flag_fields":     4,
    "partition_guard_sites":  1,
    "auto_clone_insertions":  0
  },
  "by_function": [
    {
      "function": "process_order",
      "rc_ops": 3,
      "derivation": [
        {"reason": "captured `cart` by move with two outstanding refs",
         "site": "src/order.kara:42:9"},
        {"reason": "returned from two branches with different lifetimes",
         "site": "src/order.kara:58:5"}
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

*Runtime attribution* — what fraction of wall-clock time is actually paid at each site against a real workload — is post-v1. It requires a sampling profiler over an instrumented binary; the static-count form is sufficient to answer "where does the compiler reach for fallbacks in this binary?", and the runtime-attribution form turns that into "what does it actually cost?" The static answer is the one the compiler can give without external infrastructure, so it ships first; the runtime answer is acknowledged as a follow-up so the spec stays honest about the distinction.

**`karac query monomorphization` output schema.** Returns the compiler's per-generic instantiation table — one entry per generic function, each carrying the count of monomorphizations and the resolved `(T1..Tk, E1..Em)` tuple for each instance. Surfaces the cost named in [Effect Polymorphism > Cost Properties](#cost-properties--viral-with-_-and-monomorphization-surface):

```json
{
  "scope": "src/order.kara",
  "by_generic": [
    {
      "generic": "stdlib::iter::map",
      "instance_count": 7,
      "instances": [
        {"types": ["Vec[Order]", "Receipt"], "effects": ["reads(OrderDB)"], "site": "src/order.kara:42:9"},
        {"types": ["Vec[Order]", "Summary"], "effects": ["reads(OrderDB)", "writes(Log)"], "site": "src/order.kara:51:9"}
      ]
    }
  ],
  "totals": {"generic_count": 12, "instance_count": 47}
}
```

A future `--monomorphization-budget=warn:N,error:M` flag will read this same data at compile time to enforce per-generic ceilings (warn at one threshold, error at another). The flag's default is unspecified in v1 — picking thresholds without empirical measurement against real programs would lock in numbers that turn out to be wrong (same trap as the auto-concurrency cost model). Phase 7.1 codegen produces the data that lets the default land in v1.x as a non-breaking tightening — the tracking mechanism is in v1, the policy threshold lands later.

### 3. Debugger Contract

A debugger that wants to attach to a Kāra binary needs more than DWARF/source-map fidelity: the runtime models for `par {}` blocks and `suspends`-effecting tasks have shapes that are not visible to a generic stack walker. This subsection specifies the **language-level contract** between the compiler+runtime and any debugger, profiler, or task-inspection tool. Implementing the consumer tools (gdb/lldb plugins, DAP integration, profiler GUIs, time-travel debugging) is out of scope for v1 — the v1 deliverable is the contract and the runtime metadata emission, so that consumer tools downstream have a stable surface to build against.

**Three layers, separable.** "Debugger interface" stacks three threads with different answers:

1. **Codegen debug info (DWARF, source maps).** Phase 7 hygiene — required for any debugger to attach, but not Kāra-specific. LLVM does most of the work. Not a design question.
2. **Runtime stack model under `par {}` and `suspends`.** The actual Kāra-specific contract. Specified below.
3. **Effect / RC introspection at runtime.** Useful for advanced tooling but not load-bearing. Deferred post-v1.

**The contract — four elements.** Every Kāra binary built under a profile that includes runtime debug metadata (default: `[profile.dev]` on, `[profile.release]` off; controlled by `runtime_debug_metadata = true|false` in `kara.toml`) emits the following four runtime structures, accessible through `std.runtime`:

1. **Static spawn-site IDs per `par {}` block.** Each `par {}` block in source gets a compile-time-assigned `SpawnSiteId` (a `u32` constant). The ID is per-block, not per-worker — Kāra's data-parallel model treats workers within one block as anonymous and interchangeable, so per-worker IDs would be noise. A debugger plugin that wants to identify a specific worker combines the block ID with the worker's thread-local index. Static spawn-site IDs are stable across runs of the same binary and embedded in the executable's metadata table.

2. **Parent-frame reference per worker.** Every worker frame produced by a `par {}` block, a `spawn()` call, or a `TaskGroup.spawn()` carries a pointer back to the frame that created it. This makes the structured-concurrency invariant (parent outlives children) directly observable from any child frame. The root task carries a sentinel parent-ref tagged `"root"`. Detached unstructured `spawn` is not in v1 (deferred per [§ Concurrency](#feature-5-auto-concurrency-via-effect-analysis)) — every concurrency mechanism in v1 guarantees the parent frame outlives child frames, so the contract has one shape.

3. **Await-chain pointer per suspended task.** A task suspended on an effect-boundary point (`receive`, `await`, `Sleep`, etc.) carries a pointer to the task it is waiting on (or to the I/O handle it is blocked against, surfaced as a typed `WaitTarget` enum). The pointer is per-task, not per-block: tasks have distinct awaiters and the await tree cannot be reconstructed from per-block aggregation. The asymmetry between `par` (per-block metadata) and `suspends` (per-task metadata) reflects the asymmetry of the primitives — `par` is bulk data-parallelism, `suspends` is structured task concurrency.

4. **`std.runtime::list_tasks()` and `std.runtime::list_par_blocks()`.** Two stable functions for enumerating runtime state. `list_tasks()` returns every suspended task with its `WaitTarget`, source location, and effect summary. `list_par_blocks()` returns every active `par {}` block with its `SpawnSiteId`, worker count, and per-worker source-file:line. Both functions are available in any binary built with `runtime_debug_metadata = true`; calling them in a binary built without the metadata returns an empty list (not an error — this lets generic tooling try-then-degrade).

**Profile gating.** The runtime structures cost memory and modest CPU on every spawn / suspend. Default-on for `[profile.dev]` and default-off for `[profile.release]`; programmer-overridable via the `runtime_debug_metadata` field in the active profile. Embedded and `isr` profiles are default-off — the metadata is incompatible with `panics_off` and `default_no_alloc` semantics. The profile setting is itself surfaced through `std.runtime::has_debug_metadata() -> bool`, so tooling can detect and adapt.

**Stability across compiler versions.** The four structures are part of the language-level contract and are stable within a major version. New fields may be added (additive); existing fields cannot change shape without an edition-gated migration. Tools that key on the structures see the same names, types, and meanings across compatible compiler versions.

**v1 deliverables — what ships in this phase.** The runtime metadata emission, the four contract elements above, and the `std.runtime` enumeration functions. **Out of scope for v1:** gdb/lldb plugins, DAP server, profiler GUI, time-travel debugging, replay engines. These are downstream consumer tools that build on the contract; ecosystem and engineering bandwidth, not language design, gates them. By specifying the contract explicitly now, the v1 stdlib + runtime ships with a stable surface that downstream tooling can target without forcing changes to the language or runtime later.

**Sequencing.** The Debugger Contract lands with Phase 8a auto-concurrency codegen in `roadmap.md` — that's the moment the runtime first emits `par`/`suspend` code, and the contract must be in place before then or it gets locked in by accident. Cross-reference: roadmap.md § Phase 8a.

### 4. Crash Report Format

When a Kāra program panics, the runtime emits a structured crash report. The **wire format is part of the language-level contract** (so production tooling — Sentry-class aggregators, post-mortem analyzers, automated bug filers — can dedupe and group across compiler versions); the **handler implementation lives in stdlib** (`std.panic` / `std.runtime`), overridable per Rust's `std::panic::set_hook` precedent. Mirrors the language-vs-stdlib split used for the Debugger Contract above.

**The unifying insight.** A crash report is a **serialized Debugger Contract snapshot at panic time**, plus panic-specific fields. The same metadata sources (spawn IDs, parent-frame refs, await chain, effect summaries) feed both surfaces; designing them as separate contracts would duplicate the metadata layer.

**Two output formats — both required.**

- **Structured (JSON).** The load-bearing format for tooling. Tools key on this. JSON over msgpack for v1: the speed/size of write-once-read-rarely crash files (single-digit KB) does not matter; `jq`-debuggability does ("ssh into prod, find crash file" needs zero-friction inspection); universal tooling support is on the fast ingestion path. (Streaming runtime IPC for high-frequency `list_tasks`/`get_effects` calls is a separate format decision, not the crash report.)
- **Human-readable.** Pretty-printed for terminal. Free to iterate without breaking tooling — only the structured JSON form is stable.

**Required structured fields (eight).** Every Kāra crash report contains:

1. **Panic site.** `file:line:col`, demangled function name, instruction pointer. For panics raised inside stdlib functions annotated `#[track_caller]` (the entire stdlib panic-emitter set — see [Error Handling](#error-handling) > *Stdlib panic-emitters report the caller's source location*), the recorded `file:line:col` is the **caller's** source location, not the stdlib's internal line; the demangled function name and instruction pointer continue to identify the actual emitting frame so a debugger can walk into the stdlib if needed.
2. **Panic kind discriminant.** One of `explicit` / `rc_fallback_borrow` / `par_fail_fast_cancel` / `div_by_zero` / `index_out_of_bounds` / `effect_boundary_violation` / `provider_missing` / `unreachable` (extended over time, additive). Tools dedupe on `(panic_kind, panic_site)`.
3. **Message.** The panic's payload string (or `"<no message>"` if absent).
4. **Logical stack.** Reconstructed via the Debugger Contract: per-block frames for `par`, per-task frames for `suspends`, each frame carrying source location + effect summary. Native call frames (non-Kāra C/Rust callees through FFI) appear as opaque frames with the demangled symbol but no effect data.
5. **Provider stack.** The active `with_provider` bindings at panic time — for each provider-rooted resource, which provider value was bound and at which `with_provider` site. Lets crash analyzers see which provider configuration produced the failure.
6. **RC-fallback annotations.** Any frame in the logical stack where the panic chain crosses an RC drop is annotated with the source location where the compiler chose the RC representation (drawn from the `OwnershipAnalysis` derivation chain — same data that `karac query ownership` exposes). This makes "panic in implicit `Drop` of an `Rc[Foo]`" trivially diagnosable from the crash file alone.
7. **Parallel context.** For panics inside `par {}` / `spawn()` / `TaskGroup`: which branch panicked (its `SpawnSiteId` + worker index), which siblings were still running at the moment of panic, which got cancelled by fail-fast and at what effect boundary.
8. **Build metadata.** Kāra version, compiler git commit hash, target triple, profile name, edition.

**Output discipline.**
- **Stderr** receives a 5–10 line human-readable summary plus the crash file path: enough to diagnose "what kind of failure" at a glance, plus a pointer to the full report.
- **Crash file** receives the full structured JSON. Default path `/tmp/kara-crash-{pid}-{timestamp}.json` (Unix) or `%TEMP%\kara-crash-{pid}-{timestamp}.json` (Windows); configurable via the `KARA_CRASH_DIR` environment variable. Programs that want to suppress the file (memory-only panics) set `KARA_CRASH_DIR=` (empty).
- **No partial reports.** If the report writer cannot complete the JSON serialization (out-of-disk, permission denied), it falls back to the stderr summary alone. Half-written JSON is never produced.

**Edge cases the format must address.**

- **Panic during panic-report.** The report writer itself panics (out-of-disk, allocator failure during JSON serialization). Fall back to abort + a minimal stderr line (panic site + kind only, no JSON). The runtime never re-enters the report path; it does not loop.
- **Drop-time panic.** RC drop sequences run during scope exit / unwind. The crash report **must capture drop-time panics** — this is famously broken in C++, and Kāra's no-compromise pitch demands the inverse posture. The drop frame is annotated with `panic_kind: "drop_during_unwind"` and the original triggering panic (if any) is preserved as the report's `caused_by` field.
- **Concurrent panics.** Two tasks panic simultaneously. Each writes its own crash file (filenames disambiguated by `pid`+`timestamp`+thread index); stderr lists all paths. Process exit code reflects fail-fast (the first panic to reach the scope boundary wins). The crash files cross-reference each other via `concurrent_with: [...]` arrays.
- **WASM target.** No filesystem in the browser. The structured payload is delivered to a JS-side handler hook (`window.karac_crash` by default; configurable via `KARA_CRASH_HANDLER` import in `kara.toml`); the human-readable summary goes to `console.error`. WASI targets behave like native (filesystem-backed).
- **GPU target.** GPU code cannot write disk or stderr. A panic in GPU lowering is surfaced as a host-side panic at the kernel-launch site, with `panic_kind: "gpu_kernel_failed"` and the GPU-side error code (when retrievable) in a `gpu_marker` field. Full GPU stack reconstruction is post-v1.
- **Embedded `panics_off` profile.** The format does not apply — panics are statically unreachable, the runtime's panic-report path is compiled out. Programs that compile under `panics_off` do not link against `std.panic`'s report writer at all (zero overhead, zero footprint).

**Stability.** The structured JSON wire format is stable within a major version; new fields may be added (additive, ignored by older tools); existing fields cannot change shape without an edition-gated migration. The human-readable form is free to iterate.

**v1 deliverables — what ships in this phase.** The wire-format spec (this subsection), the eight-field structured-JSON serializer in `std.panic`, the human-readable formatter, the crash-file-path convention, and the WASM/embedded edge-case behavior. **Out of scope for v1:** crash aggregator integrations (Sentry, Datadog, ELK adaptors), automated dedup/grouping CLIs, replay-from-crash-file tooling. These are downstream consumers built on the wire-format contract.

**Sequencing.** The Crash Report Format implementation lands with Phase 8a stdlib (`std.panic`, `std.runtime`) in `roadmap.md`, co-developed with the Debugger Contract because they share metadata sources. WASM and GPU adaptations land in Phase 10 alongside the respective target backends. Cross-reference: roadmap.md § Phase 8a.

### 5. Canonical Formatting

`karac fmt` produces deterministic output. **Syntactically** canonical — same scope as `gofmt` or `rustfmt`, not semantic normalization:

- Declarations canonicalized (methods sorted, imports sorted)
- Whitespace, brace placement, and ordering normalized
- AI edits produce clean semantic diffs, not reordering noise

This is syntactic canonicalization, not semantic equivalence. `a + b` and `b + a` remain as written — the formatter normalizes structure and layout, not expressions. The AST is designed for normalizability from the start.

---

## Compilation Model

Kāra is designed for AI-first code generation. Compilation frequency is high — the AI iterates rapidly and a slow compiler bottlenecks the feedback loop. The architecture commits to principles that preserve fast iteration options without requiring a full incremental compilation framework in v1.

**Principle 1: Function-local analysis by default.** Type checking, effect inference (within a strongly-connected component of mutually-recursive private functions), ownership analysis, and monomorphization requests all operate on function-sized units with explicit, named inputs. Whole-module traversals are a code smell and require justification. This shape is what a future incremental layer would key on — writing phases as whole-module sweeps now would lock out function-keyed caching forever.

**Principle 2: SCC as the natural cache unit for effect inference.** Effect inference already groups mutually-recursive private functions into strongly-connected components to reach the fixed point. That SCC is the natural invalidation unit for a future incremental layer — the implementation preserves SCC boundaries rather than flattening them away.

**Principle 3: Named dependencies between phases.** Each analysis result explicitly records what it consumed: called function signatures, in-scope resource declarations, trait impls visible at the call site, generic instantiations requested. A future incremental layer can then invalidate correctly without re-deriving the dependency graph from scratch.

**Principle 4: No numeric speed targets before measurement.** Pre-v1 there is no baseline data on Kāra compilation cost, and picking numeric targets ("100k lines/sec for type checking", "<500ms incremental rebuild") without that data produces goals that are either trivially met or impossible, with no principled basis either way. The tree-walk interpreter phase is explicitly not a performance target. First targets come from baseline measurements on non-trivial Kāra programs.

**Known-risky passes.** Three pipeline phases are called out now as the places where quadratic behavior is easiest to accidentally introduce:

- **Monomorphization.** A known compile-time-cost pain point. Kept function-local and lazy — a generic function is only instantiated on demand for the specific type arguments a call site requests, not eagerly across the monomorphization closure.
- **Effect inference SCC fixed-point.** Mutually-recursive private function clusters iterate `T_f` over the finite lattice `L` (see Feature 2 › *Effect Lattice*). The per-SCC iteration bound `n · m` — functions in the SCC times atoms in scope — keeps the pass linear in practice, and the fixed-point computation stays local to each SCC.
- **Ownership analysis with RC fallback detection.** Dataflow on each function's CFG — stays linear as long as it's function-local and does not reach across function boundaries for whole-program reachability.

**Full incremental compilation is a future optimization, not a v1 requirement.** Salsa-style dependency-tracking query infrastructure doubles compiler complexity; building it now for a prototype that is still a tree-walk interpreter is premature. The principles above exist so that layer can be added later without a pass-architecture rewrite.

**Phase names are part of the public contract.** The pipeline stages named above — `lex`, `parse`, `resolve`, `typecheck`, `effect`, `ownership`, `concurrency`, `codegen` — are the observable phases in the streaming diagnostic protocol (§ AI-First Compiler Interface > 1b. Streaming Phase Events). Renaming them is a breaking change to any AI client consuming `--output=jsonl`.

### Codegen architecture

**Codegen is the only LLVM-aware phase in the pipeline.** `src/codegen.rs` is the sole module that imports `inkwell` and references LLVM types; every upstream phase — `lex`, `parse`, `resolve`, `typecheck`, `effect`, `ownership`, `concurrency` analysis, and the tree-walk interpreter — treats the backend as a black box. AST nodes, type representations, effect records, ownership facts, and concurrency-group descriptors all live in plain Kāra/Rust data structures with no LLVM types embedded.

This is a load-bearing architectural commitment for three reasons:

1. **Backend independence at the analysis layer.** Type checking, effect inference, and ownership analysis are semantic properties of the source program, not artifacts of the chosen codegen substrate. Coupling LLVM types into those layers would entangle a backend-specific representation with language-level analysis, making the semantics harder to reason about and the analysis layer harder to test in isolation.
2. **Containment for future codegen-substrate swaps.** A migration to a different codegen substrate (e.g., MLIR — see [`deferred.md § MLIR Adoption as Codegen Substrate`](deferred.md#mlir-adoption-as-codegen-substrate)) is a **contained surgery on one module**, not a compiler rewrite. The cost of evaluating or adopting a different backend is bounded.
3. **Multiple delivery modes from one analysis pipeline.** The AOT (`karac build`) and JIT (`karac repl`, `karac test`) paths share the entire analysis pipeline; the only divergence is at codegen-output handoff (object file vs LLJIT engine). The tree-walk interpreter (development/debugging-only) reuses the same upstream phases without touching codegen at all.

**Maintainership invariant.** Future contributors must not couple LLVM types into AST-level or analysis-level structures. New phases that need to communicate codegen hints (e.g., layout decisions, vectorization annotations) do so through plain-data hint records consumed by `codegen.rs`, not through embedded LLVM types in the analysis output.

---

## Execution Model and Compilation Targets

Kāra has a single execution backend (LLVM) and two delivery modes built on it: **AOT** (`karac build` produces a native binary) and **JIT** (`karac repl` and `karac test` lazy-compile each function on first call via LLJIT). Both modes emit the same monomorphic LLVM IR — a function executed in the REPL is compiled identically to the same function in a built binary, and exhibits identical effect, ownership, and perf behavior. The tree-walk interpreter from Phase 4 is retained as a development / debugging tool but is not the runtime backend for any user-facing workflow.

### AOT — `karac build`

The default path. Source → `lex` → `parse` → `resolve` → `typecheck` → `effect` → `ownership` → `concurrency` → `codegen` → LLVM object → linked native binary via `cc`. Produces a statically-linked executable with the runtime archive (`libkarac_runtime.a`) embedded. Optimization level is configurable (`-O0` … `-O3`); default is `-O2`. CPU-feature targeting is controlled by `cpu-baseline` in `kara.toml` (default `"v3"` — see [§ Portable SIMD — Vector[T, N] > Multiversioning](#multiversioning-cpu-baseline-and-multiversion)) rather than `--target-cpu=native`, because single-binary distribution needs predictable feature requirements per build target. `--target-cpu=native` remains available as an opt-in flag for self-hosted deploys where the runtime hardware is known to match the build host; it is **not** the default. Bounds-check elision (see § Spatial safety) is what makes either path actually exercise autovectorization.

### JIT — `karac repl` and `karac test`

Same compiler pipeline as AOT, but the codegen output is loaded into an in-process LLJIT engine instead of written to an object file. Functions are compiled lazily: each function declared in a REPL session or a test module is compiled on its first call, cached for subsequent calls. There is **no hybrid tree-walk + JIT dispatch**: every user function goes through LLJIT.

**Cold-start cost.** First call to each function pays the LLJIT compile latency — measured as the v1 perf headline alongside binary size and steady-state perf. Trivial cells (`let x = 1+1`) pay the same per-cell compile cost as non-trivial cells; the cost is **uniform and expected**, not a mystery slowdown. This is by design: a known, predictable cold-start latency ("REPL has built-in compile latency") is preferable to a hybrid model where some cells run fast and others run slow with no clear rule.

**Why always-JIT, not hybrid.** A hybrid scheme (tree-walk for trivial cells, JIT for hot loops) was considered and rejected for v1. The argument: a known and uniform 5 ms vs 100 ms gap is acceptable user experience; an unexpected 2 s vs 20 s gap (when a "trivial" cell turns out to need JIT and the heuristic misses) forces the user to investigate across code, data, language, library, network, and dependencies — orders of magnitude more user pain than a flat predictable cost. Hybrid optimization remains a purely-additive future axis if real-world v1 data shows the predictable cost is unacceptable; v1 ships the simple model.

**Why LLJIT, not MCJIT or Cranelift.** Three options were considered:
- **MCJIT** (the JIT engine that `inkwell` exposes today). Feature-frozen by LLVM upstream; `clients should migrate to LLJIT` per the official guidance. Used as a sanity-check prototype only — not a shipping vehicle for v1.
- **LLJIT / ORC v2** (the modern LLVM JIT). Requires dropping to `llvm-sys::orc2` directly because `inkwell` doesn't expose it, but keeps Kāra on a single LLVM stack across AOT and JIT. **Selected for v1.**
- **Cranelift** (a separate JIT-focused codegen). ~10× faster compile than LLVM at ~14% worse code quality; would require maintaining a second backend. Deferred to v2 evaluation — Kāra's headline path is `karac build` to native, not REPL-as-primary; the cost of a dual-backend story isn't justified by v1 data.

### Crash diagnostics

**Level 2 (DWARF instruction-level, v1).** Every LLVM IR instruction is tagged with its source span via `DIBuilder`. The runtime panic handler walks the stack and resolves machine addresses to source `file:line:col` via the emitted DWARF; users see `panic at file:line:col in fn_name`. As a bonus, LLJIT/ORC's GDB JIT interface gives `gdb` / `lldb` users symbolic backtraces for free when attaching to a Kāra REPL or test process.

**Level 3 (rustc-style snippets, P1).** Builds on the same DWARF infrastructure plus a diagnostic-formatter integration: panics print rustc-style snippets with caret pointers and AST-node-aware messages (e.g., `this index operation panicked` rather than `panicked at <addr>`). Ships as a v1 patch release rather than blocking v1 itself.

### Bounds-check elision and the `get_unchecked` escape hatch

Slice and array indexing in safe code is bounds-checked at every access. Naively emitted, this places a side-exit in the middle of every loop body — which prevents LLVM's loop-optimization passes (autovectorizer, loop-invariant code motion, scalar replacement of aggregates) from firing, since their prerequisite analyses don't tolerate per-iteration exits. The result is that bumping `-O2` to `-O3` produces no measurable improvement on loop-heavy code: there is no extra room at `-O3` because the IR doesn't expose any.

Kāra addresses this with a **two-part strategy** for v1, plus a deferred third option:

**(a) LLVM-friendly bounds-check emission (P0, v1).** The compiler emits each bounds check in a form LLVM's range-analysis passes (SCEV, GVN) can prove redundant when the index is provably in-bounds from surrounding loop structure. Concrete shape: a `cmp + branch` against the slice's `len`, with the panic block marked `cold` (so it's hoisted out of the hot path) and the success-path range communicated to LLVM via `llvm.assume` annotations where appropriate. For idiomatic loops (`for i in 0..xs.len() { xs[i] }`, nested stride-based induction over slices), LLVM's existing analyses then hoist or eliminate the check entirely — the same mechanism `rustc` uses. Constant-index accesses (`xs[3]` where `3 < xs.len()` is statically known) skip the runtime check at codegen time, before LLVM ever sees the IR.

This is invisible to users — bounds checks remain semantically present in safe code; the optimization is purely a codegen shape choice.

**(c) `unsafe { xs.get_unchecked(i) }` escape hatch (P0, v1).** When the programmer knows the index is in-bounds but the compiler cannot prove it (e.g., a computed index whose validity depends on an external invariant), `Slice[T].get_unchecked(i)` provides the explicit opt-out. Same shape and semantics as Rust's `<[T]>::get_unchecked`: an `unsafe fn` returning `ref T`, with the safety contract that `i < self.len()` is the caller's obligation. The `unsafe` block at the call site documents the rationale per the `undocumented_unsafe` lint (see § Unsafe Escape Hatch). Used sparingly — most cases close under (a) — but available as the canonical recourse for the residual cases (a) misses.

```kara
// (a): idiomatic for-loop — bounds check elided by LLVM analysis
fn sum(xs: ref Slice[i64]) -> i64 {
    let mut total: i64 = 0;
    for i in 0..xs.len() {
        total += xs[i]; // bounds check present in IR; LLVM proves it redundant from loop bound
    }
    total
}

// (c): manual escape hatch when (a) can't prove the index
fn sum_strided(xs: ref Slice[i64], stride: usize, n: usize) -> i64 {
    let mut total: i64 = 0;
    for k in 0..n {
        // Safety: caller guarantees `k * stride < xs.len()` for all k in 0..n
        unsafe { total += *xs.get_unchecked(k * stride); }
    }
    total
}
```

**(b) Karac-side BCE pass (P2 contingent).** A compiler-internal pass that pattern-matches safe-indexing idioms and rewrites them to skip the runtime bounds check before LLVM codegen runs. Catches a different class of cases than (a) — patterns where the programmer knows the bound but neither the compiler's range analysis nor LLVM's reaches them. Deferred to P2 with a promotion gate: build the pass when post-v1 user data shows ≥2 distinct workload classes where (a) leaves a >1.5× perf gap. See [`deferred.md § Karac-Side Bounds-Check Elimination Pass`](deferred.md#karac-side-bounds-check-elimination-pass).

**Profile-level disable.** The `embedded` and `isr` profiles may set `bounds_checks = false` in `kara.toml` to disable runtime bounds-checking panics entirely (see § Project Profiles). This is an explicit opt-in that the profile author takes responsibility for; safe code on `app` and `lib` profiles always has runtime bounds checks (subject to (a)'s elision for provably-redundant cases).
