- **`options(volatile)`:** explicitly asserts hardware side effects even when `nomem` is present — use for instructions like `hlt`, `wfi`, `fence` that have architectural side effects beyond memory.

```kara
// This function needs writes(Hardware) — asm has memory side effects
fn disable_mmu() with writes(Hardware) {
    unsafe {
        asm("mcr p15, 0, r0, c1, c0, 0", options(nostack, preserves_flags));
    }
}

// No hardware effect needed — pure register read
fn read_cycles() -> u32 {
    let cycles: u32;
    unsafe {
        asm(
            "mrs {0}, pmccntr_el0",
            out(reg) cycles,
            options(nomem, nostack),
        );
    }
    cycles
}
```

### `global_asm`

`global_asm` emits raw assembly at file scope, outside any function. Used for:
- Interrupt vector tables
- Bootstrap / `_start` entry point
- Architecture-specific initialization before `main` runs

```kara
global_asm("
    .global _start
    .section .text
_start:
    ldr sp, =_stack_top
    bl  kara_main
");
```

`global_asm` takes no operands — it is a raw string passed through to the assembler. It is not inside `unsafe` (there is no ownership context at file scope), but it has unrestricted hardware effects by definition.

**Effect integration:** Symbols defined in `global_asm` are always accessed from Kāra code through `extern` declarations. The existing FFI effect annotation mechanism covers them — the programmer declares effects on the `extern` signature, and the compiler trusts those declarations at the FFI boundary (see [Effect defaults for `extern` functions](#effect-defaults-for-extern-functions)). No separate effect annotation on `global_asm` itself is needed.

---

**Atomic Operations and Memory Ordering** — `Atomic[T]` with `load`/`store` and the `Ordering` enum (`Relaxed`, `Acquire`, `Release`, `AcqRel`, `SeqCst`) ships in v1 to close the ISR-to-main signaling gap for embedded targets. Advanced operations (`swap`, `compare_exchange`, `fetch_add`, `fetch_and`, `fetch_or`) and hardware fences (`fence`/`compiler_fence`) are deferred to Phase 10 (P1). See [deferred.md](deferred.md) for the full specification.

---

## Standard Library Layers (`core` / `alloc` / `std`)

Kāra's standard library is split into three layers, each with strictly increasing
dependencies. This mirrors Rust's `core`/`alloc`/`std` split and enables
bare-metal targets.

**Internal SIMD usage policy.** Stdlib implementations may use `Vector[T, N]` and `#[target_feature]` paths internally where doing so provides a measurable improvement on representative workloads. The user-facing API surface remains scalar — `String.contains`, `Vec.iter().sum()`, `Map.get`, etc. all present the same scalar signatures regardless of internal vectorization. Multiversioned internal variants follow the rules in [§ Portable SIMD — Vector[T, N] > Multiversioning](#multiversioning-cpu-baseline-and-multiversion). This policy unlocks simdjson-class JSON parsing, simdutf-class UTF-8 validation, SWAR-accelerated HTTP header tokenization, Hyperscan-style regex prefilter, and similar internal optimizations — none of which are commitments, each of which follows per-workload as benchmarks justify.

### `core` — always available

Language fundamentals with zero OS or allocator dependencies:

- Primitive types (`i64`, `f64`, `bool`, `char`, pointers)
- `Option[T]`, `Result[T, E]`
- `Array[T, N]` (stack-allocated)
- Refinement types, trait definitions
- Effect system (compile-time only — no runtime overhead)
- Math (`sin`, `cos`, `sqrt`, etc.) via libm
- `format_into!` — format into a caller-supplied fixed-size buffer (no allocation)

`core` is always available, including in `no_std` + `no_alloc` kernel code.

### `alloc` — requires a heap allocator

Heap-backed types available in `no_std` projects that provide an allocator:

- `Vec[T]`, `Map[K, V]`, `String`
- `f"..."` string interpolation (produces a heap-allocated `String`)
- `shared struct` / `shared enum` (RC is heap-backed)
### `std` — requires an OS

OS-dependent functionality:

- File I/O, networking, threads, environment variables
- Channel and task runtime primitives

### Profile mapping

| Profile | `std` | `alloc` | `core` |
|---|---|---|---|
| default | yes | yes | yes |
| `embedded` | no | optional | yes |
| `kernel` | no | no | yes |

The `kernel` profile sets `no_std = true` and `no_alloc = true`. The `embedded`
profile sets `no_std = true`; `no_alloc` is opt-in (some embedded systems have
heap allocators).

### `no_alloc` formatting pattern

When `alloc` is unavailable, use `format_into!` to write formatted output into a
fixed-size stack buffer:

```kara
let mut buf: Array[u8, 64] = Array.zeroed();
format_into!(buf, "temp={} rpm={}", temp, rpm);
uart.write(buf.as_str());
```

`f"..."` interpolation requires `alloc` and is unavailable in `no_alloc` contexts.

---

## Interrupt Handler ABI (`#[interrupt]`)

Interrupt service routines (ISRs) are called by hardware, not software. They
use a different calling convention (all registers saved/restored, `iret`/`eret`
return), cannot return values, and must be placed at a specific location in the
interrupt vector table.

### `#[interrupt]` attribute

```kara
#[interrupt(TIMER1)]
fn timer1_isr() {
    TIMER1_FLAG.store(true, Release);
}
```

The argument is an identifier from a platform-defined `Interrupt` enum (defined
in the target's platform package, not the language). The compiler:

- Forces the interrupt calling convention (`extern "interrupt"` under the hood)
- Emits the correct vector table placement directive
- Forbids the function from being called directly from normal Kāra code (compile error)
- Implicitly applies the `isr` profile restrictions (no heap allocation, no
  panics) to the function body
- Infers the function's effects as `reads(Hardware) writes(Hardware)` unless
  more specific resource annotations are present

The `#[interrupt]` attribute is sugar for `extern "interrupt"` + linker placement.
Users write `#[interrupt]`; the raw `extern "interrupt"` form is available for
advanced FFI use only.

### Vector table placement

Platform packages define the `Interrupt` enum mapping vector names to positions:

```kara
// In a platform package (e.g., stm32f4)
enum Interrupt {
    TIMER1 = 28,
    UART0  = 37,
    // ...
}
```

The compiler uses the discriminant to place the ISR at the correct vector table
offset. The language does not hard-code MCU-specific vector names — all
MCU-specific knowledge lives in platform packages.

### Critical sections

Temporarily disabling interrupts uses an RAII guard:

```kara
fn update_shared_state() with writes(Hardware) {
    let _guard = critical_section.acquire();  // disables interrupts
    // ... access shared state ...
    // _guard drop re-enables interrupts
}
```

`critical_section.acquire()` returns a `CriticalSectionGuard` that re-enables
interrupts on drop. The guard type is `#[must_use]` — accidentally discarding it
immediately re-enables interrupts and is a compile warning.

---

## Codegen Hint Attributes (`#[inline]`, `#[cold]`)

Four attributes hint at code-generation choices the optimizer would otherwise make on its own. None of them changes program semantics — every program compiles to functionally identical output regardless of which subset is present. They influence performance characteristics only: code placement, call-site lowering, and inlining decisions. The hints are recorded on the function symbol and lowered to the corresponding LLVM function attribute (`alwaysinline`, `noinline`, `inlinehint`, `cold`).

| Attribute | Effect on the optimizer |
|---|---|
| `#[inline]` | Suggest inlining at call sites — non-binding. The compiler may inline or not, weighting the hint with its own cost model. |
| `#[inline(always)]` | Strong directive to inline at every call site. The compiler must inline whenever inlining is technically possible. |
| `#[inline(never)]` | Strong directive to **not** inline. The compiler keeps the function as a real call frame at every site. |
| `#[cold]` | Hint that the function is rarely executed. The compiler may move the function to a separate code section, optimize call sites for the fall-through path (assuming the call doesn't happen), and skip aggressive inlining even if `#[inline]` is present. Common shape: a slow-path error-formatting helper or panic emitter. |

**Hints, not contracts.** All four are *reported behavior*, not *guaranteed semantics* (§ Layered Specification). The compiler chooses the lowering it judges correct for the target and profile; the attributes shift the cost model, they do not control it. A function with `#[inline]` may not be inlined if the compiler decides it would hurt code size; a function with `#[inline(always)]` is not inlined at sites where inlining is **technically impossible** (see below); a function with `#[inline(never)]` may still be inlined by the linker under cross-package LTO when LTO judges it profitable. The Kāra compiler at v1 honors all four hints faithfully — `#[inline(always)]` inlines everywhere it can, `#[inline(never)]` blocks Kāra-pass inlining categorically — but the linker and the LLVM backend remain free to override at lower layers, exactly as for Rust. Programs that need a hard guarantee (e.g., to keep a frame in stack traces) cannot get it from these attributes alone; the language offers no stronger primitive at v1.

**When `#[inline(always)]` cannot inline.** Inlining is technically impossible for:

- **Recursive call sites** (a function inlined into itself either bottoms out via a base case or expands forever; the compiler picks the safe option and leaves the recursive call out of line). A non-recursive call to a `#[inline(always)]` recursive function is still inlined.
- **Function-pointer use sites** (`let f: fn() = my_fn; f()` — the indirection blocks inlining at the call). The function may still be inlined at *direct* call sites.
- **Cross-package calls without LTO** when the called function lives in a precompiled dependency that does not ship its IR. Kāra's default build pipeline ships IR for `pub` functions to support cross-package inlining; this restriction primarily matters for prebuilt vendor libraries.
- **Functions that contain blocked-from-inlining constructs** (large `asm` blocks where register allocation would interact poorly across the inline boundary; `#[interrupt]` ISR handlers — see § Interrupt Handler ABI).

In each blocked case the compiler emits no diagnostic — silently leaving the call out of line is the right behavior. Programs that depend on inlining for correctness are misusing the attribute; the language does not validate that.

**Conflict rules.** The four attributes split into two axes — inlining (`inline` / `inline(always)` / `inline(never)`) and hot/cold placement (`cold`). Within the inlining axis, only one attribute may appear per function. Violations:

- `#[inline]` and `#[inline(always)]` on the same function: `error[E_INLINE_HINT_CONFLICT]: \`#[inline]\` and \`#[inline(always)]\` are mutually exclusive`.
- `#[inline]` and `#[inline(never)]` on the same function: `error[E_INLINE_HINT_CONFLICT]: \`#[inline]\` and \`#[inline(never)]\` are mutually exclusive`.
- `#[inline(always)]` and `#[inline(never)]` on the same function: `error[E_INLINE_HINT_CONFLICT]: \`#[inline(always)]\` and \`#[inline(never)]\` are mutually exclusive`.
- `#[cold]` and `#[inline(always)]` on the same function: `error[E_COLD_INLINE_ALWAYS_CONFLICT]: \`#[cold]\` and \`#[inline(always)]\` express opposite intents`. (`#[cold]` + `#[inline]` and `#[cold]` + `#[inline(never)]` are both legal; the latter is the canonical "definitely cold, definitely keep out of line" combination.)

Repeating the same attribute on one function is `error[E_DUPLICATE_ATTRIBUTE]` (covered by the general attribute checker).

**Where they may appear.**

| Position | Allowed |
|---|---|
| Free functions (`fn`) | Yes |
| Methods inside `impl` blocks | Yes |
| Methods inside `impl Trait for T` blocks | Yes |
| Trait method *declarations* | Yes — the hint applies to every impl unless that impl carries its own override (last-writer-wins, parallels `#[track_caller]` propagation). |
| Closures and lambdas | No — closures are inlined by the compiler when they cross a closure-typed parameter (`Fn`-trait dispatch is monomorphized inline by default). The attributes are rejected on closure expressions with `error[E_CODEGEN_HINT_ON_CLOSURE]: codegen hints attach to named functions; closures are inlined by the dispatch lowering`. |
| `extern "C" fn` definitions | Yes — applies to the Kāra-side body the same way as a normal function. |
| Foreign-function declarations inside `unsafe extern { ... }` | No — there is no Kāra-side body to inline; the compiler cannot reach inside a foreign symbol. Rejected with `error[E_CODEGEN_HINT_ON_EXTERN_DECL]`. |
| `impl Drop for T` destructors | Yes — Kāra-pass inlining of destructors is governed by the same hints; no special case. |
| Entire `impl` block (apply to every method) | No at v1. The attribute attaches per-method. (Block-level shorthand is post-v1.) |

**No semantic effect on the effect system.** Inlining and cold placement are purely codegen concerns; the inlined body's effect set has already been incorporated into the caller's inferred effects through normal effect inference, and the hint does not change the inferred set. A `#[cold] fn report_error(e: ref Error) with writes(Log) { ... }` inlined or not still contributes `writes(Log)` to its caller's effects.

**No interaction with `#[track_caller]`.** A `#[track_caller]` function carries a hidden caller-location parameter; this is orthogonal to inlining decisions. A `#[track_caller] #[inline]` function may be inlined; when inlined, the caller-location parameter is folded away (the receiver function's site replaces the hidden parameter). When not inlined, the caller-location is passed normally. Both attributes coexist without diagnostic.

**Diagnostic for misapplication on bodies whose inlining never happens.** When a `#[inline(always)]` function's only call sites are dynamic (every call is through a `fn`-pointer), the compiler at v1 emits no diagnostic — the silent-leave-out-of-line rule applies. Future tooling (`karac build --perf-report`) will surface "`#[inline(always)]` had no inlining sites" as a hint, but the build itself never fails on it.

---

## Linker Control Attributes

Three attributes control how symbols appear in the linker's view of the binary.
Two of them — the ones that pick a symbol name or place a symbol at a
programmer-specified linker location — carry obligations the type system
cannot verify (symbol-name uniqueness, section validity, layout/aliasing
constraints), so they must be written in the **`#[unsafe(...)]` wrapping
form**. The third (`#[used]`) only suppresses dead-code elimination on its own
and stays plain.

The `let` and `let mut` bindings below are module-level bindings in the sense
of [Module-Level Bindings](#module-level-bindings): their initializers are
compile-time constant expressions (array literals and `[0; N]` repeat forms),
so they satisfy the compile-time initializer rule without special cases.

| Attribute | Purpose | Wrapping form |
|---|---|---|
| `#[unsafe(link_section("name"))]` | Place the symbol in a named linker section | `#[unsafe(...)]` required |
| `#[unsafe(no_mangle)]` | Use the Kāra identifier as-is; disable name mangling | `#[unsafe(...)]` required |
| `#[used]` | Prevent dead-code elimination even if no Kāra code references the symbol | plain (no wrap) |

The bare forms `#[link_section(...)]` and `#[no_mangle]` are rejected at parse
time with a focused diagnostic suggesting the `#[unsafe(...)]` wrap. The wrap
is a visual trust-boundary marker — a reviewer scanning for everything that
can violate symbol uniqueness or layout assumptions can grep for `#[unsafe(`.

```kara
// Interrupt vector table at a fixed address (ARM Cortex-M)
#[unsafe(link_section(".vectors"))]
#[used]
let INTERRUPT_VECTORS: Array[*const fn(), 256] = [...];

// Stable export name for bootloader / OS loader
#[unsafe(no_mangle)]
pub extern "C" fn kernel_main() { ... }

// DMA buffer in fast SRAM (cache-line aligned for DMA)
#[unsafe(link_section(".dtcmram"))]
#[repr(align(32))]
let mut dma_rx_buffer: Array[u8, 512] = [0; 512];
```

**Definitions vs. imports.** The `extern "C" fn name(...) { ... }` form
above (with a body) is a Kāra function exposed under a foreign ABI — it is a
*definition*, not a *foreign import*. Definitions keep their syntactic shape;
their soundness-affecting attributes (`#[unsafe(no_mangle)]`,
`#[unsafe(link_section(...))]`) carry the trust-boundary load. Foreign
imports — declarations of foreign symbols with no Kāra-side body — must live
inside `unsafe extern "ABI" { ... }` blocks instead; see [FFI](#ffi). When the
defined function may panic, the boundary semantics are determined by the chosen
ABI (`"C"` auto-aborts; `"C-unwind"` propagates) — see [Panic Semantics at the
FFI Boundary](#panic-semantics-at-the-ffi-boundary).

### `#[unsafe(no_mangle)]` vs ABI

`#[unsafe(no_mangle)]` controls only the *symbol name* — it does not imply
`extern "C"` ABI. A function can have `#[unsafe(no_mangle)]` with Kāra
calling convention (linker can find it by name, but callers must know the
ABI) or with `extern "C"` for C interop. Both annotations are needed when a
bootloader expects a C-ABI entry point with a stable name.

Kāra mangles names by default (to support generics and avoid cross-module
collisions). `#[unsafe(no_mangle)]` disables this for the annotated item.
The wrapping is mandatory because the programmer is asserting the chosen
symbol name does not collide with any foreign symbol in the final binary —
an obligation the compiler cannot check across the FFI boundary.

### `#[used]` and dead-code elimination

The compiler performs dead-code elimination on unreferenced globals. `#[used]`
marks a symbol as externally referenced (by hardware, a linker script, or a
foreign loader) and suppresses elimination. Required for interrupt vector tables,
linker-placed constants, and any symbol that hardware will access by address.

`#[used]` stays plain (no `#[unsafe(...)]` wrap) because on its own it has no
soundness impact — it only suppresses DCE. A `#[used]` symbol that is mangled
and not exported by name remains unreachable from outside the binary; the
worst case of an over-applied `#[used]` is dead bytes. The attributes that
*do* expose the symbol (`#[unsafe(no_mangle)]`, `#[unsafe(link_section)]`)
carry their own wrap; layering `#[unsafe(used)]` on top would be ceremony
without information.

### Build-profile linker flags

The attributes above operate at the per-symbol level. Two related concerns operate at the **build-profile** level — they affect every symbol the linker sees, set in `kara.toml` or via build-flag defaults rather than at individual definitions:

**Strip + dead-code elimination.** Release builds run `strip -x` on the linked binary (drops `__LINKEDIT` symbol tables, ~18% size reduction on macOS) and pass linker DCE flags (`-Wl,-dead_strip` on macOS, `-Wl,--gc-sections` on Linux + ELF builds with `-ffunction-sections -fdata-sections`). The Mach-O linker has function-level dead stripping always-on via `.subsections_via_symbols`, so macOS gets per-function DCE by default; Linux ELF requires the explicit flags. Cross-archive LTO (`lto = "thin"` in the runtime crate's release profile) gives the linker visibility into runtime-internal symbols for further DCE.

**Panic strategy and unwinding tables.** `panic = "abort"` in the runtime crate's release profile drops `__eh_frame` and `__unwind_info` sections (~114 KB on a representative binary). Default for `embedded` / `kernel` / `isr` / `gpu` profiles, where unwinding is forbidden anyway. App / lib profiles use `panic = "unwind"` by default to preserve `catch_panic` semantics; users can switch to `panic = "abort"` per-build for additional binary-size savings, accepting the loss of `catch_panic` (which becomes a compile error under `abort`).

These flags are profile-driven, not per-symbol. Soundness obligations stay at the symbol level via the `#[unsafe(...)]` attributes above. The build-profile flags are a separate layer concerned with binary footprint and panic-strategy uniformity, not with symbol-name uniqueness or section validity. Implementation tracking lives in [`implementation_checklist/phase-7-codegen.md`](implementation_checklist/phase-7-codegen.md).

**Cross-references:** § Project Profiles (where `panic = "abort"` defaults are set per profile); § Panic Strategy (the unwind / abort semantic split); § Catching Panics (`catch_panic[T]` requires `panic = "unwind"`).

---

## C Calling Convention Variants

The general form is `extern "<abi>"`. Kāra supports the following ABIs:

| ABI string | Used for |
|---|---|
| `"C"` | Standard C ABI (default for FFI) |
| `"C-unwind"` | C/C++ functions that may propagate exceptions |
| `"stdcall"` | Win32 API (Windows kernel/driver) |
| `"fastcall"` | x86 register-passing convention |
| `"win64"` | Windows x64 ABI |
| `"sysv64"` | System V AMD64 ABI (Linux/macOS x64) |
| `"interrupt"` | ISR entry points; used by `#[interrupt]` internally |

For the first release, `"C"` and `"C-unwind"` are implemented. The others are
reserved names — specifying them produces a "not yet supported" compile error
rather than silently falling back to `"C"`.

### `extern "C-unwind"` and the effect system

`extern "C-unwind"` declares that the function may propagate a C++ exception
across the FFI boundary. **`panics` is defaulted by the ABI** — selecting
`"C-unwind"` is itself the declaration that the function may unwind, so the
compiler adds `panics` to the declaration's effect set automatically. The
programmer does not write `panics` explicitly on the `extern "C-unwind"`
signature; it is implied. See [Effect defaults for `extern` functions](#effect-defaults-for-extern-functions)
for the full default table. Calling such a function still requires `panics` in
the caller's effect set — the default only spares the `extern` declaration from
redundancy, not the caller from propagation.

**Absorbing the panic at the FFI boundary.** A C++ exception that crosses an
`extern "C-unwind"` boundary into Kāra surfaces as a Kāra panic with
`PanicKind.Foreign`. To absorb it without forcing the entire caller chain
to declare `panics`, wrap the FFI call in [`catch_panic[T]`](#catching-panics-catch_panict)
— the standard panic-absorption primitive (per v60 item 26):

```kara
unsafe extern "C-unwind" {
    fn cpp_may_throw(x: i32) -> i32;    // panics is defaulted by the ABI
}

fn caller_propagating() with panics {
    let result = cpp_may_throw(42);     // ok — caller declares panics
}

fn caller_absorbing() -> Result[i32, PanicInfo] {
    catch_panic(|| cpp_may_throw(42))   // panics absorbed → PanicInfo with kind Foreign
}
```

The unwinding-runtime substrate that makes both `catch_panic` and the
caller-propagating form work lands with the LLVM backend (Phase 7); under
the `panic = "abort"` build profile, `extern "C-unwind"` is rejected at
parse — there is no unwinding infrastructure to propagate exceptions
through, and a C++ throw across the boundary would be undefined behavior.

### Panic Semantics at the FFI Boundary

The `panics` effect interacts with the FFI boundary in four cases — two for *imports* (calling C from Kāra) and two for *exports* (Kāra functions called from C). The cases differ along two axes: which ABI the boundary uses (`"C"` vs `"C-unwind"`), and which direction the call flows.

| Case | ABI | Direction | Behavior on panic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `extern "C"` | C → Kāra (export) | Kāra runtime catches the unwind at the boundary and aborts the process. Body's `panics` effect describes Kāra-side calls; the wrapper is invisible to Kāra callers. |
| 2 | `extern "C-unwind"` | C → Kāra (export) | Body's panic propagates across the boundary as a C++-exception-shaped unwind. C++ callers may catch; C callers see process termination. |
| 3 | `extern "C"` | Kāra → C (import) | C function may `abort` / `exit` / `longjmp` / install signal handlers — surfaces as `panics` if and only if the programmer declared it. The compiler trusts the declaration (existing rule). |
| 4 | `extern "C-unwind"` | Kāra → C (import) | C function may throw a C++ exception across the boundary; `panics` is defaulted by the ABI selection. The exception surfaces to Kāra as a panic with `PanicKind.Foreign` (per [Catching Panics](#catching-panics-catch_panict)). |

Cases 3 and 4 are the existing rules for foreign imports — see [Effect defaults for `extern` functions](#effect-defaults-for-extern-functions). Cases 1 and 2 are the *export* rules: what happens when a Kāra function is exposed to C and the Kāra body panics.

**Case 1 — `extern "C" fn` exports auto-abort on panic.** When a Kāra function is defined as `pub extern "C" fn name(...) { ... }` and the body panics during a C-originated call, the runtime catches the in-flight unwind at the boundary and aborts the process via `std.process.abort()`. This is *defined-abort*: the failure mode is committed and observable, not undefined behavior.

```kara
pub extern "C" fn double(x: i32) -> i32 {
    x.checked_mul(2).unwrap()              // body may panic on overflow
}
// Called from C — panic on overflow becomes process abort with the panic message on stderr.
// Called from Kāra — panic propagates normally; C-side wrapper is invisible.
```

The wrapper is implemented as a compiler-generated trampoline: codegen emits the bare Kāra function under one symbol (callable directly from Kāra) and a separate exported wrapper symbol (callable from C) that wraps the call in a `catch_panic`-equivalent and routes any caught panic to the abort path. Kāra-side calls go through the bare symbol with no overhead; C-side calls go through the trampoline. Tooling that reads the symbol table sees both names — the C-facing one is the documented entry point; the bare Kāra-callable one is an implementation detail.

The exported function's signature **may** declare `panics`. The declared effect describes the *Kāra-side* call path (where panics propagate normally); the C-side trampoline is invisible to the effect system. Declaring `panics` is honest about the body's behavior and does not change the C-boundary contract.

```kara
pub extern "C" fn parse_or_die(input: ref String) -> i32 with panics {
    parse(input).unwrap()                  // declared panics for Kāra callers
}                                          // C callers still see auto-abort on panic
```

**Case 2 — `extern "C-unwind" fn` exports propagate across the boundary.** When a Kāra function is defined as `pub extern "C-unwind" fn name(...) { ... }` and the body panics, the unwind is permitted to cross the FFI boundary and surfaces on the C side as a C++ exception. Callers written in C++ that wrap the call in a `catch (...)` block can recover; callers written in C see process termination at the next runtime call into a function with no exception handler. This is the opt-in form: the choice of `"C-unwind"` ABI is itself the declaration that the function may unwind.

```kara
pub extern "C-unwind" fn parse_or_throw(input: ref String) -> i32 with panics {
    parse(input).unwrap()                  // panic propagates to the C++ side
}
```

The exported function's signature **must** declare `panics` if the body may panic — the ABI's whole purpose is to admit unwinding across the boundary, and silently absorbing a body panic would defeat the point. The compiler enforces this with `error[E_EXTERN_C_UNWIND_REQUIRES_PANICS]` when the body's inferred effects include `panics` but the declaration omits it.

**Surfacing errors to C without aborting.** When a Kāra `pub extern "C" fn` body wants to communicate a recoverable error to C rather than abort, the supported pattern is to wrap the body's logic in [`catch_panic[T]`](#catching-panics-catch_panict) and translate the result to a C-shaped error code:

```kara
pub extern "C" fn parse_safe(input: *const u8, len: usize, out: *mut i32) -> i32 {
    catch_panic(|| {
        let s = unsafe { String.from_raw_parts(input, len) };
        unsafe { *out = parse(s).unwrap() };
        0                                  // success
    })
    .map_err(|info| {
        log::error(f"parse_safe panicked: {info.message}");
        -1                                 // C-shaped error code
    })
    .unwrap_or_else(identity)              // collapse Result[i32, i32] → i32
}
```

The auto-abort trampoline still wraps the body — it is the safety net for panics that escape the user-written `catch_panic` (e.g., a panic during the closure that runs the panic-handler logic itself). Programmers who write the explicit `catch_panic` pattern get fine-grained error reporting; programmers who omit it get auto-abort with a stderr message.

**Build-profile interaction.** Under `panic = "abort"` (kernel/embedded/ISR profiles), every panic is already a process abort — the auto-abort trampoline is redundant and the codegen elides it. The `panic` build profile and the FFI-boundary behavior compose as follows:

| Build profile | `extern "C" fn` (export) | `extern "C-unwind" fn` (export) |
|---|---|---|
| `panic = "unwind"` (default) | Auto-abort trampoline catches panic at boundary | Permitted; panic propagates to C++ |
| `panic = "abort"` | Trampoline elided (panic is already abort) | **Rejected at parse** — no unwinding infrastructure; `error[E_EXTERN_C_UNWIND_REQUIRES_UNWIND_PROFILE]` |

The same `panic = "abort"` rule rejects `extern "C-unwind"` *imports*, and rejects `catch_panic` calls — see the build-profile note in [Catching Panics](#catching-panics-catch_panict). All three reject under the same profile because none of them have anything to operate on without unwinding tables.

**Effect-system composability.** The four cases in the table compose with the rest of the effect system without any special-case rules:

- An imported `extern "C-unwind"` function carries `panics` by default; calling it transitively contributes `panics` to the caller's inferred set, which the caller then either propagates (declaring `with panics`) or absorbs via `catch_panic`.
- An exported `extern "C" fn` body's inferred effects, including `panics`, are computed normally; the boundary trampoline is a codegen-only artifact and contributes no effect to the function's signature.
- An exported `extern "C-unwind" fn` body must declare `panics` if it may panic; the declaration is the public contract and the C-side ABI signal align by construction.
- The `panic = "abort"` build profile interacts with declarations the way it always does — `panics` declarations are still permitted, but every panic compiles to abort, so the boundary distinctions evaporate.

### `extern "interrupt"` and `#[interrupt]`

`extern "interrupt"` is the low-level ABI form. `#[interrupt(NAME)]` is sugar
that generates an `extern "interrupt"` function and emits the correct vector
table placement. Users should use `#[interrupt]`; `extern "interrupt"` is
available for cases where the attribute abstraction is insufficient — for
example, when the interrupt vector is not listed in the platform package's
`Interrupt` enum (custom hardware, bootloader trampolines), or when vector
table placement is handled by an external linker script rather than the
compiler. Like every other foreign-import declaration, the raw form lives
inside an `unsafe extern "interrupt" { ... }` block (see
[`unsafe extern { ... }` block requirement](#unsafe-extern---block-requirement)).
`#[interrupt]` itself, applied to a Kāra function with a body, is a
*definition* and stays plain — the compiler-generated lowering produces an
appropriately-placed function whose ABI and section the compiler can verify
from the platform package's `Interrupt` enum.

---

## FFI

C-compatible ABI. Foreign-import declarations — names of foreign symbols with no Kāra-side body — must live inside an `unsafe extern "ABI" { ... }` block; the bare standalone form `extern "C" fn write(...);` is rejected at parse time. Resource effects on these declarations are the programmer's responsibility; see [Effect defaults for `extern` functions](#effect-defaults-for-extern-functions) for what the compiler defaults and what must be annotated:

```
unsafe extern "C" {
    fn write(fd: i32, buf: *const u8, count: usize) -> isize
        with writes(FileSystem);
}
```

### `unsafe extern { ... }` block requirement

Every foreign-import declaration in Kāra lives inside an `unsafe extern "ABI" { ... }` block. Two item kinds are legal inside the block: foreign function declarations (`fn name(...) -> T with E;`) and opaque foreign type declarations (`type Foo;` — see [Opaque Foreign Types](#opaque-foreign-types-extern-c--type-foo-)). The `unsafe` keyword is **not** an `unsafe { }` expression — it is a keyword on the `extern` block itself, marking the declarations inside as a trust boundary the compiler cannot verify. The block author is asserting that:

- the declared signatures match the foreign symbol's actual ABI shape;
- the declared effects faithfully describe what the foreign code does (per the trust-not-verify rule below);
- the foreign symbol exists at link time and points to the function the declaration claims it does;
- any opaque type declared in the block names a real C type that the C side guarantees pointers to are valid.

None of these are checkable by the compiler — they are the foreign-language contract the Kāra side must trust. Forcing every foreign-import declaration to live inside an `unsafe extern { }` block makes that trust boundary visually unmissable in source. A reviewer scanning a module knows that everything inside an `unsafe extern { }` block is a foreign contract and everything outside is checked.

**Calling foreign functions does not require an `unsafe { }` expression.** The unsafe-trust assertion lives at the *declaration site* (the `unsafe extern { }` block); call sites are ordinary expressions. Effects declared on the foreign function flow through the caller's effect set normally. This is the deliberate asymmetry with [`unsafe fn`](#unsafe_op_in_unsafe_fn-rule): an `unsafe fn` declares a precondition that callers acknowledge at *every* call site, while an `unsafe extern { }` block is a trust assertion made *once* at the declaration — calls into FFI are not themselves unsafe operations once the declaration is trusted.

**Standalone shorthand removed.** The earlier (pre-v1) shorthand `extern "C" fn name(...);` for a single-declaration foreign import is removed from the grammar. Single-import cases use the block form with one item:

```
unsafe extern "C" {
    fn getpid() -> i32;
}
```

The two extra lines are deliberate ceremony — there is no shape difference between binding one foreign function and binding a hundred. The cluster discipline encouraged by the block form (related foreign symbols grouped under one trust boundary) is the intended idiom.

**Definitions are not affected.** A Kāra function exposed under a foreign ABI (`extern "C" fn kernel_main() { ... }` — *with* a body) is a *definition*, not a foreign import. The body is type-checked normally; the soundness-affecting attributes (`#[unsafe(no_mangle)]`, `#[unsafe(link_section(...))]`) carry the trust-boundary load instead. See [Linker Control Attributes](#linker-control-attributes).

### Effect defaults for `extern` functions

The compiler has no body to analyze when it encounters an `extern` declaration. Two rules govern what happens to the declaration's effect set: a small set of ABI-keyed defaults, and a strict trust-not-verify rule for everything else.

**Default effect sets by ABI:**

| ABI | Default effect set | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `extern "C"` | `{blocks}` | `#[noblock]` opts out for pure-CPU helpers. Resource effects: empty. `panics` not defaulted. |
| `extern "C-unwind"` | `{blocks, panics}` | `#[noblock]` still available. Resource effects: empty. `panics` is implied by the ABI selection itself — see [`extern "C-unwind"` and the effect system](#extern-c-unwind-and-the-effect-system). |

Resource effects — `reads`, `writes`, `sends`, `receives`, `allocates` — default to the **empty set** for both ABIs. Resource identity is a Kāra-side concept that does not exist in C; there is no reasonable default, so the programmer must annotate any resource effect the foreign function performs against Kāra-side resources. `suspends` is never inferred for FFI (§[Leaves for `suspends`](#execution-effect-inference)).

**`panics` is not defaulted on plain `extern "C"`.** A C function that may `abort`, `exit`, `longjmp`, or terminate via a signal handler requires an explicit `panics` annotation. The rationale: plain `"C"` carries no unwinding intent in its ABI, so the compiler cannot infer `panics` from the ABI alone — the programmer must read the C documentation and declare the behavior. `"C-unwind"` is the opposite case: selecting the `"C-unwind"` ABI is itself a declaration that the function may unwind across the boundary, so requiring a redundant explicit `panics` would be ceremony without information.

**Trust-not-verify at FFI boundaries.** All effect annotations on `extern` declarations — whether defaulted or written by the programmer — are treated by the effect checker as *declarations of record*, not hypotheses to verify. This is the explicit exception to the general rule at [Effect Inference and Boundaries](#effect-inference-and-boundaries) that declared and inferred effects must match: for FFI there is no inferred side, because there is no body. A declaration of `fn foo() with reads(Network);` inside an `unsafe extern "C" { ... }` block contributes `reads(Network)` to every caller's inferred set, but the compiler does not verify that `foo` actually reads the network. A stale or incorrect annotation on an `extern` function is the programmer's responsibility at the FFI trust boundary — and the `unsafe` keyword on the enclosing block is the visible record of that responsibility.

```kara
// Compiles fine even though the C function touches a filesystem, not a socket.
// FFI annotations are trusted declarations — the compiler cannot see foo's body.
unsafe extern "C" {
    fn foo() with reads(Network);
}
```

The `karac` linter still pattern-matches known-blocking or known-allocating libc entries and suggests annotations (see the over-declare paragraph below), but pattern matches are advisory hints, not enforcement. Callers that depend on FFI effect correctness for data-race-freedom or profile compatibility are ultimately trusting the programmer who wrote the `extern` declaration — the standard FFI trust boundary at any language/native interface.

**Why the defaults are small.** `{blocks}` is present because a false no-blocks produces silent thread starvation (§[FFI default rationale](#execution-effect-inference)) — the asymmetry between over-conservative scheduling (recoverable) and under-conservative scheduling (unfixable at runtime) makes `blocks` the safe default. `{panics}` is present on `"C-unwind"` because the ABI is the declaration. Nothing else gets a default, because no other verb has an ABI-visible signal that the compiler can key off.

**`#[noblock]` attribute.** `#[noblock]` is a per-declaration attribute on `extern` functions that removes `blocks` from the function's default effect set. It is placed before the `fn` keyword inside an `unsafe extern { }` block:

```kara
unsafe extern "C" {
    #[noblock]
    fn abs(x: i32) -> i32;
}
```

Use `#[noblock]` for pure-CPU C helpers (`abs`, `strlen`, math routines, hash functions) that are guaranteed to never park the thread. The attribute is trusted at the FFI boundary — the compiler does not verify that the foreign function truly never blocks. An incorrect `#[noblock]` on a function that does block produces silent thread starvation, so it should only be applied when the programmer has verified the C function's behavior. `#[noblock]` has no effect on resource verbs or `panics` — it removes only `blocks`. `#[noblock]` is **not** in the `#[unsafe(...)]` wrap list — it is an effect-set modifier inside an already-unsafe extern block, not a soundness/layout/linking attribute on its own.

**Block-level `#[noblock]`.** To avoid per-function boilerplate when binding a CPU-only library, `#[noblock]` may be applied to an entire `unsafe extern { }` block:

```kara
#[noblock]
unsafe extern "C" {
    fn sqrt(x: f64) -> f64;
    fn compress(buf: *const u8, len: usize) -> *mut u8;
    fn crc32(data: *const u8, len: usize) -> u32;
}
```

All declarations inside inherit `#[noblock]`. A single declaration inside the block may override this with an explicit `blocks` annotation if one function in the library does block. The block-level form is the idiomatic way to bind pure-computation C libraries (math, compression, hashing) without repeating `#[noblock]` on every declaration.

**Effect declarations on `extern` are programmer-supplied beyond the defaults.** The compiler has no body to analyze, so the programmer must name every additional verb that applies, including execution and allocation verbs that are easy to overlook:

- **`blocks`** on any C function that may park the OS thread — `sleep`, `read` on a blocking fd, `pthread_mutex_lock` under contention, synchronous DNS lookups, any libc call that waits on a kernel object. If in doubt, declare it: undeclared blocking in an `async` worker pool is the canonical way to freeze a Kāra runtime.
- **`panics`** on any C function that may abort, longjmp out, or call a registered signal handler that terminates the process — `abort`, `exit`, `assert`, functions that dereference user-supplied pointers, anything invoking `setjmp`/`longjmp`. Unwinding semantics at the FFI boundary follow the `extern "C-unwind"` rules; `panics` declared on plain `extern "C"` means the function may terminate the program, not that it may unwind through Kāra frames.
- **`allocates(Heap)`** on any C function that calls `malloc`/`calloc`/`realloc` directly or transitively — most non-trivial C APIs (`getaddrinfo`, `strdup`, `fopen`, almost all parser libraries). Under the default profile this is permitted by default and rarely surfaces, but it matters in `embedded`/`kernel` profiles where `allocates(Heap)` is forbidden — an FFI call into a heap-using C function there is a compile error, which is exactly the desired behavior.

The general rule: when in doubt, over-declare. A too-liberal effect set on an `extern` function costs the caller some parallelization or profile compatibility; a too-narrow one is unsound and can silently break the runtime model. This is a guideline, not a compile-error rule — FFI annotations are trusted declarations (see *Trust-not-verify at FFI boundaries* above), so the compiler cannot reject an under-declared `extern` signature. The `karac` linter flags common omissions when the function name pattern-matches known-blocking or known-allocating libc entries, but pattern matching is a hint, not a substitute for the programmer reading the C documentation.

FFI calls always run on real OS threads regardless of runtime model. Standard library wraps raw extern declarations in safe typed functions.

### FFI Unions: `union Foo { ... }`

C unions — the type whose storage is shared by every named field rather than partitioned among them — exist in Kāra solely as an FFI shape. They are spelled with the `union` keyword as a top-level item, take `#[repr(C)]` for C-ABI compatibility, and require `unsafe { }` at every field *read*.

```kara
#[repr(C)]
union FloatBits {
    f:    f32,
    bits: u32,
}

let x = FloatBits { f: 3.14 };          // construction is safe — exactly one field is named

let raw: u32 = unsafe { x.bits };        // read is unsafe — reinterprets f32 storage as u32

x.bits = 0x40490FDB;                     // write of a Pod field is safe — no interpretation
```

**Why a separate kind from `enum`.** Kāra's tagged `enum` already covers the "one of several shapes" use case at the language level — it carries a discriminant, the typechecker proves which variant is live, and the borrow checker tracks per-variant payloads. A C union is the *opposite*: the variant is *not* tracked; the bytes are reinterpreted on every access. The two should not share a syntax. `union` is the FFI-only shape for binding C headers that expose untagged unions (`union sigval`, `union epoll_data`, x86 register-context structs, audio/video codec descriptors), reading hardware registers whose meaning depends on bits elsewhere, and the niche bit-twiddling cases where `transmute` would be the alternative.

**Declaration form.**

```
UNION_DECL = ATTRS "union" IDENT "{" UNION_FIELDS "}"
UNION_FIELDS = UNION_FIELD ( "," UNION_FIELD )* ","?
UNION_FIELD  = VIS? IDENT ":" TYPE
```

A `union` must have at least one field. Empty unions are rejected at parse with `error[E_EMPTY_UNION]`. Tuple-style unions (`union Foo(i32, u32)`) are not provided — every field must be named so reads reference the field by name rather than by index.

**Required `#[repr]`.** Every `union` must carry `#[repr(C)]` (or `#[repr(C, packed)]`). A bare `union Foo { ... }` without a `#[repr]` declaration is rejected at typecheck with `error[E_UNION_REQUIRES_REPR]: \`union\` requires #[repr(C)] — without an ABI annotation a union has no defined layout`. The Rust-style "default repr" is deliberately omitted: unions exist only for FFI, and committing to a layout without naming it is exactly the footgun this whole feature is meant to prevent.

**Layout.** `size_of` is the max of the field sizes; `align_of` is the max of the field alignments; size is rounded up to a multiple of the alignment. Padding bytes between the union's start and its end (when the largest field is shorter than the rounded-up size) are uninitialized — reading them is unsafe and reinterprets unspecified memory.

**Field constraints.** Every field type must be `Copy` (i.e., must satisfy the `Pod`/`Copy` trait — see [Conversion Traits](#conversion-traits-from-into-tryfrom-tryinto)). Non-`Copy` field types are rejected at typecheck with `error[E_UNION_FIELD_NOT_COPY]`. The rule covers two soundness footguns at once:

1. **Drop ordering.** A union with a `Drop` field cannot be dropped soundly — the language doesn't know which variant is live, so it cannot decide whose `drop` to run. Forbidding `Drop` fields removes the question.
2. **Write semantics.** Writing a `Copy` field to a union is a pure bit-overwrite — it cannot drop the previously-stored variant (because the previous bytes were `Copy` too, with no destructor). The rule keeps writes safe.

The `Copy` constraint is checked at union-declaration time, not at use site, so an `unsafe` workaround does not exist. Programs that need a non-POD payload reach for `enum` (the right tool for tagged variants) or wrap the field in `MaybeUninit[T]` and accept the unsafe-init / unsafe-deinit dance per-variant.

**Construction.** A `union` is constructed with a struct-literal-shaped expression naming exactly one field:

```kara
let bits = FloatBits { f: 3.14 };           // OK
let bits = FloatBits { bits: 0x40490FDB };  // OK
let bits = FloatBits { f: 1.0, bits: 1 };   // ERROR — exactly one field per construction
let bits = FloatBits {};                    // ERROR — exactly one field per construction
```

`error[E_UNION_LITERAL_REQUIRES_ONE_FIELD]` covers both the multi-field and zero-field cases. The single-field rule is what makes construction *safe*: the bytes are written with a known interpretation, so no reinterpretation is happening at the construction site.

**Field access semantics.**

| Operation | Safety |
|---|---|
| Read a field — `let v = u.field` | `unsafe { }` required |
| Take a reference to a field — `let r = &u.field` / `&mut u.field` | `unsafe { }` required |
| Take a raw pointer — `ptr.const(u.field)` / `ptr.mut(u.field)` | safe (per [Raw Pointer Construction](#raw-pointer-construction-ptrconst--ptrmut)) |
| Assign to a field — `u.field = v` | safe (`Pod` field, no drop) |
| Pattern-match on a field — `let Foo { field } = u` | `unsafe { }` required |

The asymmetry between assignment-safe and read-unsafe is the same rule as Rust's: writing a `Copy` field commits to a fresh interpretation; reading any field reinterprets bytes whose interpretation the compiler cannot verify.

**Forbidden interactions.**

- **No generics.** `union Foo[T] { ... }` is rejected at parse. Layout depends on the largest field, and a polymorphic union would have to compute layouts per monomorphisation; that is feasible but rolling it into v1 buys nothing the FFI use case requires (every C union has a fixed shape). Lifting this restriction later is additive.
- **No `Drop` impl.** `impl Drop for Foo` on a union type is rejected at typecheck (the field-`Copy` rule forbids fields that need dropping; a user-supplied `Drop` impl would be the only way to introduce one and is therefore forbidden too).
- **No automatic derives.** `#[derive(...)]` on a union is rejected at parse. None of the builtin derives have well-defined semantics on a union (`Eq` / `Hash` / `Debug` would have to pick a field, `Clone` is provided implicitly because every field is `Copy`, `PartialEq` over raw bytes is a reasonable but surprising default we do not commit to). Custom `impl` blocks for user-defined traits are permitted.
- **No `#[non_exhaustive]`.** A `#[non_exhaustive]` union would have a hidden field, making `size_of` non-public — incoherent with the FFI use case.

**Effect integration.** Union field reads are `unsafe { }`-only but contribute no effects to the enclosing function's inferred effect set on their own. Reading reinterpreted bytes is a *language-level* unsafe operation; if the read triggers a hardware effect (an MMIO register read, a volatile read), that is captured by the surrounding `unsafe` block via the existing `volatile_read` / `reads(Hardware)` machinery — not by the union read itself.

**Cross-`extern` use.** Unions appear in two FFI shapes:

1. **As a field of an `extern "C"` struct or as a function argument/return.** No special handling — the C ABI rules in [`#[repr]`](#repr--abi-and-physical-layout-control) cover layout, and the `#[repr(C)]` requirement on the union is what makes the ABI shape defined.
2. **Imported from a C header.** The Kāra `unsafe extern "C" { }` block does not contain `union` declarations directly — `union` is a *type definition* and lives outside the extern block. The pattern is to declare the `#[repr(C)] union Foo { ... }` as a top-level type and reference it from the `unsafe extern { }` block's signatures, mirroring the pattern for `#[repr(C)] struct`.

```kara
#[repr(C)]
union epoll_data {
    ptr: *mut u8,
    fd:  i32,
    u32: u32,
    u64: u64,
}

#[repr(C)]
struct epoll_event {
    events: u32,
    data:   epoll_data,
}

unsafe extern "C" {
    fn epoll_wait(epfd: i32, events: *mut epoll_event, maxevents: i32, timeout: i32)
        -> i32 with reads(Network) writes(Network);
}
```

**`MaybeUninit[T]` is the better answer when one exists.** Unions exist for binding *given* C shapes. New Kāra code that needs "I have storage, I will fill it later" should reach for [`MaybeUninit[T]`](#uninitialised-memory-maybeuninitt-only) — it is the typed wrapper for the same problem and gives the compiler more information.

### Opaque Foreign Types: `extern "C" { type Foo; }`

Many C APIs hand a Kāra program a *handle* — a pointer to a struct whose layout the C library does not publish. `FILE*`, `xmlNode*`, `sqlite3*`, `EGLDisplay`, `cairo_surface_t*`, the GTK widget hierarchy: each of these is an opaque pointer whose interior is private to the implementing library. Kāra binds them with an *opaque foreign type declaration* inside an `unsafe extern { }` block:

```kara
unsafe extern "C" {
    type FILE;

    fn fopen(path: *const u8, mode: *const u8) -> *mut FILE
        with reads(FileSystem) writes(FileSystem);

    fn fclose(stream: *mut FILE) -> i32
        with writes(FileSystem);

    fn fread(buf: *mut u8, size: usize, count: usize, stream: *mut FILE) -> usize
        with reads(FileSystem);
}
```

**`type FILE;`** declares `FILE` as an unsized type whose size, alignment, and field layout are unknown to Kāra. It is *not* a definition — there is no `{ ... }` body, no fields, no methods, no derives. The C side guarantees the type exists and that the pointers Kāra receives are valid; Kāra treats every `*mut FILE` as an opaque handle.

**Why a separate kind from `struct` / `union`.** A Kāra `struct` requires fields; it has a known layout. A `union` requires `#[repr(C)]` and field-`Copy` constraints; it has a known size. An opaque foreign type has *no known layout at all* — the compiler cannot allocate one on the stack, copy one by value, or compute its size. The only operations the type supports are *being pointed to*. Modelling this with a phantom-field struct would either lie about the size (UB on `size_of`) or force a fictional layout (defeating the point — the C library can change its layout across versions). The dedicated declaration form makes the contract honest.

**Permitted operations.**

| Operation | Status |
|---|---|
| Pointer types: `*const Foo`, `*mut Foo` | Permitted everywhere a pointer type is permitted. |
| Reference types: `ref Foo`, `mut ref Foo` | Permitted; references are sized pointers and the borrow-checker rules apply normally. |
| FFI function signatures using the above | Permitted as parameter, return, and field types whenever the type appears behind a pointer or reference. |
| `ptr.const(...)` / `ptr.mut(...)` on a place of type `Foo` | **Not** permitted — there is no place of type `Foo` to take the address of in safe Kāra code; opaque types only enter the program as `*mut Foo` returned from FFI. The compiler rejects `ptr.const(some_foo)` at typecheck because no such binding can exist. |

**Forbidden operations.** Each rejected at typecheck with a focused diagnostic naming the rule:

- By-value bindings: `let f: FILE = ...` — `error[E_OPAQUE_TYPE_REQUIRES_INDIRECTION]: opaque foreign type 'FILE' has no known size; values of opaque types must appear behind a pointer or reference (\`*mut FILE\`, \`ref FILE\`, \`mut ref FILE\`)`.
- By-value parameters or returns: `fn f(x: FILE)` / `fn f() -> FILE` — same diagnostic.
- As a field type in a non-FFI struct: `struct S { f: FILE }` — same diagnostic. (As a field type *behind a pointer*, `struct S { f: *mut FILE }`, is fine.)
- Field access: `foo.x` on `*mut FILE` (after the implicit deref) — `error[E_OPAQUE_TYPE_NO_FIELDS]`. The compiler does not know whether a field exists, so it rejects access uniformly.
- Method calls: same diagnostic. (Inherent or trait methods cannot be defined on the opaque type because Kāra cannot see its layout to monomorphise them.)
- `size_of[FILE]()`, `align_of[FILE]()` — `error[E_OPAQUE_TYPE_NO_KNOWN_SIZE]`. The compiler has no value to return.
- Construction: there is no syntax that names `FILE` as a value, so this case is structurally impossible to write — no dedicated diagnostic is needed.
- Pattern matching against an opaque-type place — same as field access; the matched value's structure is unknown.
- `#[derive(...)]` and `impl Trait for Foo` blocks: the latter is rejected with `error[E_OPAQUE_TYPE_NO_INHERENT_OR_TRAIT_IMPLS]`. The opaque type is for the C side; trait conformance must come through a *wrapper* type defined in Kāra (e.g., a `distinct type SafeFile = *mut FILE` plus `impl Drop for SafeFile { ... }`).
- Generics: `extern "C" { type Foo[T]; }` — rejected at parse with `error[E_OPAQUE_TYPE_GENERIC_FORBIDDEN]`. C does not have generic types; if the binding needs to be generic on Kāra's side, the wrapper type carries the parameter.

**Visibility and scope.** An opaque-type declaration carries the same visibility surface as any other item — `pub`, module-internal, or package-internal. The type's name is a regular type-namespace identifier; importing it via `use lib.FILE` works. Multiple `unsafe extern { }` blocks may declare the *same* opaque type name only if they all refer to the same underlying C type (the compiler checks this by name equality at link time — duplicate declarations are flagged as `warning[W_OPAQUE_TYPE_REDECLARED]` rather than rejected, since the C side has the final word).

**Drop and destructors.** Opaque types have no `Drop` impl, no destructor, and the language never calls one on a `*mut Foo`. C-side cleanup goes through the explicit FFI function (`fclose(file_ptr)`); a Kāra wrapper type usually owns the pointer and routes its `Drop` impl to call the C cleanup function. The wrapper-Kāra-`Drop` calls-foreign-cleanup pattern is the idiom; it composes with `defer` and with the `panics` effect on the cleanup function.

```kara
distinct type SafeFile = *mut FILE;

impl Drop for SafeFile {
    fn drop(mut ref self) {
        if !ptr.is_null(self.raw()) {
            unsafe { fclose(self.raw()) };  // declared effects flow through normally
        }
    }
}

fn open_file(path: ref String) -> Result[SafeFile, IoError] {
    let raw = unsafe { fopen(path.as_ptr(), c"r".as_ptr()) };
    if ptr.is_null(raw) { return Err(IoError.from_errno()); }
    Ok(SafeFile(raw))
}
```

**Effect integration.** Opaque type declarations themselves carry no effects — they are type definitions. The functions inside the same `unsafe extern { }` block that take or return pointers to the opaque type follow the standard FFI effect-declaration rules ([Effect defaults for `extern` functions](#effect-defaults-for-extern-functions)). The trust-not-verify boundary applies: the Kāra side trusts the declared effects on the FFI functions that produce / consume the opaque pointers; the opaque type contributes nothing on its own.

**Cross-language / cross-version stability.** Because the layout is unknown, an opaque foreign type declaration is *layout-version-independent*: the C library can change `FILE`'s internal fields between releases without breaking Kāra binders that only use `*mut FILE`. This is the entire point of opaque handles in C APIs and the reason this form is worth carving out as a distinct kind from `struct`. A consumer that wants layout stability commits to it explicitly with `#[repr(C)] struct SomeKnownLayout { ... }`.

**`extern_types` is opt-in to the opacity model.** When the C library *does* publish the layout — through a `.h` header that exposes the struct's fields — Kāra binders should declare the layout explicitly with a `#[repr(C)] struct` and gain the field-access surface. Opaque types are for the cases where the layout is intentionally hidden by the library author. Mixing the two on the same type (`#[repr(C)] struct FILE { ... }` *and* `extern "C" { type FILE; }`) is a coherence error, flagged at link time when the duplicate name is detected.

---

## Host Functions

A `host fn` declaration names a function **provided by the compilation host** — the foreign JS environment on browser-WASM, the WASI / Component-Model runtime on server-WASM, a C-ABI library on native. It is the single language-level surface for host-bound functions; the compiler handles target-specific lowering so library authors write one declaration and the binding works on every target.

### Why `host fn` exists alongside `extern`

`extern "C"` is Kāra's raw C-ABI door. It takes a foreign symbol name, C-ABI-shaped parameters, and produces a direct call on native. On WASM the programmer must hand-write JS glue or a C shim to make it usable.

`host fn` is one layer higher. The declaration is target-neutral; the compiler lowers:

- **Native:** to an `extern "C"` call of the same signature (identical code path to a plain `extern`).
- **Browser-WASM:** to a WASM `import` entry under the **`kara_host`** import-module namespace, with compiler-generated JS glue (wasm-bindgen-compatible calling convention so existing JS tooling works). Shipped — see "Browser-WASM lowering (v1 shape)" below.
- **Server-WASM (WASI / Component Model):** under the default `--bindings component`, to a WIT-backed import — the canonical-ABI instance `kara:<pkg>/host` with kebab-case function names, matching the `interface host` the embedded world declares; the Component Model host supplies the implementation at component instantiation. Under `--bindings none` / `browser`, the C-ABI shape remains: the same `kara_host` import entry as the browser target, where the embedder's import object is the thin shim. No glue file is generated either way. Both shipped — see "Server-WASM lowering (v1 shape)" below. The user-facing `host fn` surface is identical across all shapes.

A library author declares a browser API once as `host fn`; the same source file compiles against all three targets.

### Syntax

```kara
host fn dom_append(parent: ElementHandle, child: ElementHandle)
    with writes(Display);

host fn fetch_begin(url_ptr: *const u8, url_len: i64) -> RequestHandle
    with sends(Network), receives(Network), suspends;
```

No body — ends with `;`. Placed at module scope. Supports attributes and visibility like any other item. (`ElementHandle` / `RequestHandle` are opaque-handle newtypes and the string crosses as a `(ptr, len)` pair — earlier drafts showed `url: ref String` / `-> Result[Bytes, ...]` here, which the restriction list below rejects; a richer `fetch_json(url: ref String)` is the *library wrapper* built on top, not the `host fn` itself.)

### Effect declarations are required

Every `host fn` **must declare its effects** with a `with` clause. The compiler has no body to analyze, so the effect set is a trusted declaration of record — the same trust-not-verify rule as `extern "C"` at [Effect defaults for `extern` functions](#effect-defaults-for-extern-functions). Callers inherit the declared effects via the normal inference rules.

There is **no `host fn` effect default**. `extern "C"` carries an implicit `{blocks}` default tied to the C ABI's blocking-syscall model; `host fn` targets a heterogeneous host set (browser event loop, WASI, native C) and no single ABI-level default fits. The programmer declares every effect explicitly, including `blocks` / `suspends` / `panics` when they apply.

Stdlib modules (`std.web`, `std.wasi`) exemplify correct `host fn` effect declarations — `fetch` declared with `sends(Network), receives(Network), suspends`; DOM mutations declared with `writes(Display)`; timer callbacks declared with `writes(Timer)`.

### Parameter and return types

`host fn` parameters and return types are restricted to:

1. **Primitive types** — integer types, float types, `bool`, `char`, `()`, pointer types.
2. **`Copy` types** — user-defined types that satisfy `Copy` (small structs of primitives).
3. **Opaque-handle newtypes** — user-defined single-field structs wrapping a primitive, used to give stronger typing to host-allocated resources (DOM nodes, file descriptors, GPU buffer IDs). An opaque handle is an ordinary Kāra struct; the host identity lives in its single scalar field.

**Not permitted in v1:**

- **Owned non-`Copy` types across the boundary.** Moving a heap-owned Kāra value across a host call raises aliasing and ownership-transfer questions that do not compose cleanly with JS or WASI host code.
- **`ref T` / `mut ref T` parameters.** Borrow aliasing rules are a Kāra-compiler property; the host cannot be asked to honor them.
- **Generic `host fn` declarations.** Monomorphizing across the host boundary requires cooperation from the host-side binding layer that is not a solved problem in 2026.

Library authors build richer ergonomics on top of `host fn` — a user-facing `fn dom_set_text(el: ref Element, text: ref String)` is an ordinary Kāra function whose body calls `host fn` primitives after converting the arguments into the restricted form.

### Ownership across the boundary

Opaque handles do **not** participate in Kāra's RC. A handle is a scalar; copying it does not increment any reference count, and dropping it does not run a destructor. Resource lifetime on the host side is the host's responsibility — libraries wrapping host APIs provide safe lifecycle management (e.g., an `Element` struct with a `host fn element_release(handle)` call in its `Drop` impl) as user-space code.

### Relationship to `extern "C"`

`extern "C"` is not removed or deprecated. It remains the low-level primitive for direct C-ABI binding and for cases where the programmer does not want compiler-mediated lowering. On native, `host fn` and `extern "C"` produce the same call sequence for equivalent signatures; on WASM, `extern "C"` is available but requires hand-written glue for any non-trivial use. `host fn` is the recommended surface for anything that needs to be target-portable.

### Lowering summary

| Target | Lowering |
|---|---|
| Native | `extern "C"` call (identical code path to a hand-written `extern "C"` with the same signature). |
| Browser-WASM | WASM `import` entry under the `kara_host` namespace + generated JS glue (wasm-bindgen-compatible calling convention). Shipped — v1 shape below. |
| Server-WASM (WASI / Component Model) | Default (`--bindings component`): WIT-backed import under the canonical-ABI `kara:<pkg>/host` instance, supplied at component instantiation. `--bindings none`/`browser`: WASM `import` entry under the `kara_host` namespace, implementations supplied by the embedder at instantiation (the C-ABI shape). Both shipped — v1 shape below. |

The lowering layer lives in the compiler (Phase 10). The user-facing `host fn` surface is stable across all three paths; migration from the interim import-entry shape to Component Model for server-WASM is non-breaking at the source level.

### Browser-WASM lowering (v1 shape)

`karac build <file>.kara --target=wasm_browser` emits `<stem>.wasm` plus `<stem>.js`, a zero-dependency ES-module glue file. The committed v1 decisions:

- **Browser modules are wasm32-wasip1 modules.** The browser target reuses the `wasm_wasi` module flavor — same runtime archive, allocator unification, and entry-shim chain — and the generated glue supplies a minimal console-backed WASI preview-1 polyfill (`fd_write` → `console.log`/`console.error`, `proc_exit`, clock, randomness via `crypto.getRandomValues`; un-polyfilled syscalls throw loudly by name). A WASI-free `wasm32-unknown-unknown` flavor is a possible post-v1 refinement; the glue API is the stability boundary, not the module flavor.
- **`kara_host` is the import-module namespace** for every `host fn` — a stable contract. The glue maps the user's implementation object onto it and rejects instantiation loudly (naming the functions) when an implementation is missing; hand-rolled hosts that skip the glue instantiate with `{ kara_host: {...}, wasi_snapshot_preview1: {...} }`. Plain `extern "C"` declarations do **not** get import entries — an unresolved one stays a hard link error.
- **JS boundary types:** `i64`/`u64`/`isize`/`usize` cross as `BigInt`; every other legal scalar (including wasm32 pointers) is a JS number. Opaque handles cross **at their declared scalar width** — an i32-field handle is a number, an i64-field handle a BigInt. Strings cross as `(ptr, len)` scalar pairs; the glue exports `readString(memory, ptr, len)`, and every host implementation receives one trailing context argument `{ memory, readString }` so string params decode without plumbing the memory export by hand.
- **Loader compatibility:** the glue's default loader is `new URL("<stem>.wasm", import.meta.url)` — the asset-reference pattern vite / webpack / esbuild / rollup rewrite without custom configuration — with a `node:fs` branch for `file:` URLs (the same glue runs under node ≥ 18) and a compile-from-bytes fallback when a server mis-types the wasm MIME. `instantiate(hostImpls, opts)` accepts `opts.module` / `opts.bytes` to bypass the loader; `run(hostImpls)` drives `_start` and swallows a clean exit.

### Server-WASM lowering (v1 shape)

`karac build <file>.kara --target=wasm_wasi --bindings none` (and the browser shape) lowers `host fn` declarations to the **same `kara_host` import entries** as the browser target — one C-ABI contract across those shapes. A core-wasm import entry *is* a C-ABI call resolved by the host, and the embedder-side implementation is the thin shim. The committed v1 decisions:

- **No glue file.** Server-WASM hosts are heterogeneous (wasmtime, node, jco, edge runtimes) and each has a first-class import-object/linker surface already; generated glue would pick a winner. The embedder instantiates with both namespaces — `WebAssembly.instantiate(mod, { ...wasi.getImportObject(), kara_host: {...} })` under node, `linker.func_wrap("kara_host", "<name>", ...)` under wasmtime.
- **Boundary types are core-wasm types.** The contract is the module's import signatures: i64/u64/isize/usize as wasm `i64`, everything else (including wasm32 pointers) as `i32`/`f32`/`f64` scalars; strings cross as `(ptr, len)` pairs read from the exported linear memory. How those surface in the host language is the host's convention (JS hosts: `i64` ↔ `BigInt`, pointers as numbers — same as the browser glue documents), not part of the Kāra contract.
- **`extern "C"` differential preserved:** plain `extern "C"` declarations get no import entries on either WASM target — an unresolved one stays a hard undefined-symbol link error. Only `host fn` opts into host-provided resolution.
- **Component Model migration path — discharged.** The promised swap landed with the embedded-WIT migration, at exactly the single compiler site that attaches the import attributes: under `--bindings component` the same declarations lower to `wasm-import-module = "kara:<pkg>/host"` with kebab-case `wasm-import-name`s (the canonical-ABI strings the embedded world declares — both sourced from the same `wit.rs` helpers so they cannot drift). Source-level `host fn` declarations and their effect contracts were unchanged by the swap, as committed.

---

## What Kāra Is Not

Explicit non-goals. These are deliberate design boundaries, not missing features.

**No named lifetime parameters.** No `'a`, no `'b: 'a`, no lifetime bounds in generic signatures, no lifetime-like disambiguation annotations. Kāra uses owned returns by default, explicit `ref` for borrow returns, and RC fallback for the rest. When a function has multiple `ref` parameters and returns a borrow, the compiler conservatively assumes the return may borrow from *all* `ref` parameters — no annotation needed, no expressiveness gap worth the cognitive load. For borrowed struct returns, the compiler infers the borrow set by tracing `ref` fields to source parameters when the body is visible; when the body is opaque (public function from another module), the same conservative rule applies — the return is assumed to borrow from all `ref` parameters. No annotation is ever needed.

**No `async fn`. No function coloring. No yield-point syntax.** There is no `async` keyword on function declarations, and no `.await` or equivalent at call sites. A function that performs network I/O looks identical to a pure computation — the programmer writes `let resp = http_get(url)?` and the compiler handles everything: inferring the `suspends` effect, inserting yield points, transforming the function into a state machine, and routing the task to the async worker pool. The `suspends` effect propagates through the call graph like any other effect — no call-site syntax and no function-coloring keyword are needed. Public functions still declare `suspends` explicitly like any other effect, per the standard effect boundary rules. This is a complete departure from other languages where `.await` marks yield points and `async fn` colors function signatures — in Kāra, the effect system replaces both.

**No garbage collector.** Ownership + RC fallback provides deterministic deallocation. No GC pauses, no runtime scanning, no stop-the-world collection.

**No runtime cycle collector.** Cycles are detected at compile time via type graph analysis. Back-edges use `weak`. For `dyn Trait` in `shared struct`, the `#[cyclic]` annotation enforces `weak` at compile time; a debug-mode leak detector catches missed cases (compiled out in release — zero production overhead). For performance-critical graph workloads, `Arena[T]` is available as a stdlib primitive.

**No algebraic effect handlers.** No Koka-style delimited continuations. Effects are a static analysis tool for the compiler, not a control flow mechanism. Runtime flexibility comes from trait-based provider injection, not effect interception.

**No field-level effect granularity.** `writes(UserDB)` is the finest granularity for unparameterized resources. No `writes(UserDB.email)` — it leaks schema into signatures, is brittle, and most databases don't guarantee field-level isolation. Parameterized resources (`UserDB[user_id]`) provide row-level partitioning when needed.

**No compiler auto-detection of SoA layouts.** The compiler does not automatically transform AoS to SoA. Layout decisions are explicit and opt-in via `layout` blocks. `karac explain` bridges the gap: it statically analyzes field access patterns — identifying which fields of a collection appear together in tight iteration loops — and emits a concrete, ready-to-paste `layout` block with `group` and `cold` annotations as a diagnostic. No code is changed without the programmer writing the block. The programmer decides; `karac explain` makes the decision tractable.

**No classes or inheritance.** Composition via structs + traits + impl blocks. No vtable hierarchies, no method resolution order, no diamond problem.

**No exceptions.** Errors are values (`Result[T, E]` with `?` operator). `panics` is for truly unrecoverable situations (out of memory, assertion failures). No hidden control flow from try/catch.

**No implicit parallelism of conflicting effects.** The compiler never parallelizes operations that conflict on the same resource. Conservative serialization is always the default. The programmer opts into finer parallelism via parameterized resources or `independent` declarations.

**No universal-purpose pitch.** Kāra is a systems language for explicit intent, safe implicit concurrency, and compiler-assisted performance. It is not trying to replace Python, JavaScript, or Java for their core use cases.

---

## Deferred Items

**Priority notation.** P0 = committed (Phases 1–9), blocked on prerequisite work. P1 = decided and non-breaking, deferred to post-MVP (Phase 10+). P2 = open/speculative, may never ship. Phases 1–8 are the MVP compiler; Phase 9 is Gradual Verification Enforcement (post-MVP, P0). Phase numbers refer to the implementation roadmap in `docs/roadmap.md`.

Items below are **P0** — they are committed but blocked on prerequisite work. For post-MVP features (P1: decided/non-breaking, P2: speculative), see [deferred.md](deferred.md). Detailed design specs for items with committed designs follow the summary table.

| Item | Depends on | Target | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Comptime / const generics | Working compiler | Phase 7-8 | Zig-inspired compile-time code execution. Enables custom derives, compile-time validation, code generation in same language. Effect-system placeholder decisions committed — see [Comptime Effect Defaults](#comptime-effect-defaults) below. |
| `Concurrent` `marker trait` (entry retitled) | `par struct` in use | Phase 10 (P1) | **Superseded by item 48's closed-enumeration approach.** Originally specced as an auto-trait propagating concurrent-safety through the type graph; per [No Auto-Traits](#no-auto-traits) (v60 item 61), Kāra rejects auto-derivation as a mechanism. If `Concurrent` ships at all, it lands as an explicit `marker trait` with `impl Concurrent for T { }` user / compiler code at the `par struct` / `par enum` declaration site, *not* as an auto-trait. The structural cross-task-safe set committed in [Structured Concurrency Lifetime Guarantees](#structured-concurrency-lifetime-guarantees) already covers the cross-task safety story without `Concurrent` shipping at all; whether the explicit marker is needed for generic bounds (`fn broadcast[T: Concurrent](...)`) is a Phase 10 decision. The "auto-trait" wording is preserved here for historical context only. |
| Interior mutability optimization | Effect system battle-tested | Phase 7-8 | `shared struct` `mut` fields use per-field runtime borrow tracking (see [Shared Types](#part-5-shared-types)). Revisit UnsafeCell optimization once the effect system has proven its aliasing coverage. |
| Self-referential structs | Ownership stability | Phase 7-8 | `shared struct` or Arena workaround available. |
| Generators (`yield`) | Traits + generics | Phase 10 (P1) | `yield` keyword reserved. Desugars to `Iterator` implementations. **Orthogonal to `suspends`:** `yield` is a pure iteration mechanism — it does not produce the `suspends` effect and does not interact with the concurrency runtime. `suspends` is concurrency-only (compiler-inferred from call graph). Manual `Iterator` covers v1. |
| User-defined operator overloading | v1 operator traits shipped | Phase 8 | v1 dispatches `a + b` through `Add.add`. Deferred piece is lifting restriction on `impl Add for UserType`; purely additive. |
| Channel combinators (select, fan-out/fan-in, unbounded) | Concurrency runtime | Phase 6 | v1 specifies the basic `Sender[T]` / `Receiver[T]` bounded-channel primitive (see [Channels](#channels)). Deferred: `select` across multiple channels, channel-based fan-out/fan-in combinators, unbounded channels, and the broader async message-passing story. `Mutex[T]` with `lock` block syntax is decided. `Atomic[T]` moved to Phase 10 (P1) — see [deferred.md](deferred.md). |
| Numeric / SIMD auto-vectorisation | LLVM backend | Phase 7 | LLVM auto-vectorises scalar loops where possible. Explicit SIMD uses `Vector[T, N]` — see *Portable SIMD* below. |
| Build system / package manager | Module system, FFI | Phase 7-8 | `kara.toml` manifest committed (see [Package System](#package-system)). Remaining: centralized registry, resolver algorithm, build CLI. |
| Arena[T] stdlib type | Standard library | Phase 8 | Optional performance primitive for bulk allocation. |
| Complexity budgets (G43) | Profiles (G46) | Phase 10 (P1) | Heap case covered by effect system. Stack budget requires transitive call graph analysis. Merged with Static Stack Depth Analysis in `deferred.md`. |
| Effect variable bounds | Effect inference stable | Phase 10 (P1) | Upper-bound constraints on effect variables: `with E: no writes(R)`. See [Effect Variable Bounds](#effect-variable-bounds) below. Unbounded `with E` sufficient for MVP. |
| Per-SCC effect diagnostics (enhanced) | Effect checker | Phase 5 | Full effect resolution trace for mutual recursion groups. See [Per-SCC Effect Diagnostics](#per-scc-effect-diagnostics) below. |
| Portable SIMD `Vector[T, N]` | LLVM backend | Phase 7+ | `Vector[T: Numeric, const N: i64]` — single portable type for CPU SIMD and GPU vectors, with automatic scalar fallback when the target lacks a vector unit. See [Portable SIMD — `Vector[T, N]`](#portable-simd--vectort-n) below. |
| Perceus in-place reuse | LLVM backend | Phase 7+ | Best-effort RC allocation reuse. See [Perceus-Style In-Place Reuse](#perceus-style-in-place-reuse) below. |
| Yielding subscripts | Coroutines | Phase 7+ | Coroutine-based `[]` access as complement to `Index`/`IndexMut`. See [Yielding Subscripts](#yielding-subscripts) below. |
| Generational fallback annotation | Pool[T] in use | Phase 4+ | `#[generational_fallback]` as opt-in per-type annotation. See [Generational References](#generational-references) below. |
| `if let` chains | Basic `if let` in use | Phase 5+ | Chained `if let` with `and`. See [`if let` Chains](#if-let-chains) below. |
| Bitfield types | `#[repr]` in use | Phase 5+ | `#[repr(C, bitfield)]` with sub-byte fields. See [Bitfield Types](#bitfield-types) below. |
